proposals.title: Interlingua Induction via Denoising and Synthetic Parallelism: Learning Cross-Lingual Summarization Representations from Monolingual Data
proposals.motivation: High-quality parallel data for summarization across languages is scarce, limiting the development of robust cross-lingual summarization systems. This scarcity is a major bottleneck for creating equitable NLP tools for low-resource languages. Existing methods heavily rely on sentence-aligned corpora, which are expensive and often unavailable for summarization-specific tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an interlingua representation for summarization can be effectively learned primarily from monolingual documents and summaries by combining denoising autoencoder objectives with synthetic parallel data generated via iterative back-translation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a multilingual encoder-decoder transformer (e.g., mT5 or mBART) pre-trained on denoising objectives. The core method involves a two-stage process: first, fine-tuning the model on monolingual summarization tasks in multiple languages separately to learn language-specific compression. Second, we will employ an iterative back-translation loop, where the model generates summaries in a pivot language (e.g., English) for non-English documents, and these synthetic (document, summary) pairs are used to train a unified cross-lingual summarizer. We will incorporate faithfulness evaluation metrics from STORYSUMM to guide and filter the synthetic data generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for building cross-lingual summarization systems without parallel summarization data, significantly expanding their potential applicability. It will also produce a new diagnostic dataset and framework for evaluating the quality of the induced interlingua, specifically measuring content preservation and factual consistency across languages.
proposals.novelty: While back-translation is established for machine translation, its application to learn an interlingua for the more abstract task of summarization from purely monolingual data is novel. The integration of fine-grained faithfulness evaluation directly into the training loop to curate synthetic data distinguishes this from prior work on unsupervised machine translation.

proposals.title: Evaluating Interlingua Summarization for Low-Resource Languages: A Faithfulness and Pluralistic Alignment Study
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) for summarization are predominantly evaluated on high-resource languages, leaving their performance on diverse, low-resource languages poorly understood. This gap is critical because summarization tools are needed globally, and prior work, such as STORYSUMM and PERSONA, highlights the importance of evaluating faithfulness and pluralistic alignment—dimensions that may degrade significantly in low-resource settings. A systematic investigation is required to assess whether interlingua approaches, which leverage cross-lingual transfer, can maintain content quality and mitigate biases for languages with limited parallel data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an interlingua summarization approach, fine-tuned with fine-grained supervision on a mix of high- and mid-resource languages, will demonstrate robust but variable performance on low-resource languages, with faithfulness errors and persona-alignment biases correlating with linguistic distance from the training set and available monolingual data quantity.
proposals.method: We will extend the fine-grained supervision framework from 'Aligning Large Language Models via Fine-grained Supervision' to a massively multilingual setting. Using a model like mT5 or a multilingual T0 as a base, we will create a training corpus spanning typologically diverse languages (e.g., Arabic, Swahili, Bengali, Turkish) with available summarization data, plus synthetic or few-shot data for very low-resource languages (e.g., Oromo, Quechua). We will adapt the STORYSUMM faithfulness metrics and the PERSONA testbed for multilingual evaluation, automatically translating persona prompts and source documents while using native speakers for quality assessment on a subset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of interlingua summarization across a broad spectrum of resource levels, quantifying trade-offs in faithfulness and pluralistic alignment. It will produce a public dataset and testbed for low-resource language summarization evaluation, directly extending the STORYSUMM and PERSONA frameworks to a global context.
proposals.novelty: While multilingual LLMs exist, their systematic evaluation for summarization-specific tasks like faithfulness and persona-based alignment across a deliberate gradient of resource availability is novel. This moves beyond aggregate performance metrics to diagnose specific failure modes in low-resource scenarios.

proposals.title: Aligning Zero-Shot Translation via Fine-Grained, Faithful Supervision from Pivot-Based Traces
proposals.motivation: Zero-shot multilingual translation in large language models (LLMs) offers operational simplicity but suffers from a significant quality gap compared to pivot-based translation (translation via an intermediate language like English). This gap limits the practical utility of LLMs for many language pairs, especially low-resource ones. Bridging this gap is crucial for building efficient, high-quality, and equitable multilingual NLP systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the quality gap stems from a lack of fine-grained, faithful supervision for direct language transfer, and that distilling high-quality, pivot-based translation trajectories into the model can provide the necessary signal to significantly improve zero-shot translation faithfulness and fluency.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel dataset of parallel translation 'traces': for a source-target pair (e.g., Japanese-Korean), we will generate a high-quality pivot translation (Japanese->English->Korean) and annotate it for faithfulness against the source, inspired by STORYSUMM evaluation methods. We will then fine-tune a multilingual LLM (e.g., a variant of LLaMA or BLOOM) using a fine-grained supervision objective that aligns the model's internal representations and decoder outputs for the direct zero-shot path (Japanese->Korean) with the faithful, pivot-based trajectory. This extends PERSONA-like pluralistic alignment to translation paths and employs contrastive learning to suppress common zero-shot biases (e.g., over-reliance on English lexical patterns).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel methodology for improving zero-shot translation via pivot-based distillation, 2) a new diagnostic dataset for evaluating translation faithfulness across direct and pivot paths, and 3) empirical evidence on the specific linguistic phenomena that cause the zero-shot gap, advancing the understanding of multilingual alignment in LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While pivot-based translation is a known strong baseline, using its internal reasoning traces as fine-grained supervisory signal for zero-shot alignment is novel. This approach uniquely combines insights from faithfulness evaluation (STORYSUMM) and pluralistic alignment (PERSONA) specifically for the translation task.

proposals.title: InterSum: Leveraging Interlingual Representations for Faithful Cross-Lingual Summarization Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual summarization (CLS) is crucial for global information access, but its evaluation remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026, standard metrics like ROUGE are unreliable across languages, and human evaluation is costly and non-scalable. There is a pressing need for automated, faithfulness-focused evaluation methods that can operate robustly across language pairs, directly addressing the core themes of summarization, evaluation, and dataset creation in your prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language-independent (interlingua) representation space, learned via fine-grained supervision on parallel text, can serve as a robust substrate for directly comparing source documents and their cross-lingual summaries, enabling more accurate and faithful automated evaluation.
proposals.method: We will first extend the fine-grained supervision approach from your alignment work to build an interlingua encoder using massively multilingual parallel corpora (e.g., from OPUS). We will then apply this encoder to the CLS evaluation task: for a source document and its candidate summary in another language, we encode both into the shared interlingua space. Faithfulness will be measured by the semantic similarity between these representations, using metrics like cosine similarity or a learned regression head trained on human judgments of faithfulness from datasets like XL-Sum. We will benchmark against translation-based baselines and analyze biases introduced by the interlingua.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, faithfulness-oriented automatic metric for cross-lingual summarization, 2) a new diagnostic dataset for CLS evaluation highlighting cross-lingual factual consistency errors, and 3) an analysis of how interlingua representations capture or distort semantic content across diverse languages, advancing methods for content-based evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While interlingua ideas exist in machine translation, their application to the specific problem of cross-lingual summarization *evaluation* is novel. This approach bypasses error-prone translation pipelines and directly leverages your expertise in fine-grained supervision and faithfulness evaluation (STORYSUMM) for a multilingual context.

proposals.title: Modular Multilingual Scaling: Parameter-Efficient Architectures for Massively Multilingual Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) struggle to scale efficiently to dozens or hundreds of languages, as naive dense scaling leads to parameter explosion, catastrophic interference, and high inference costs. This is a critical bottleneck for building equitable NLP systems, particularly for summarization tasks where cultural and linguistic nuances are paramount. Pre-2026, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA are primarily applied to single languages or small multilingual sets, leaving their efficacy and interaction in massively multilingual regimes unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modular, compositionally designed architecture using language-specific and task-specific PEFT adapters will enable efficient scaling to hundreds of languages while maintaining or improving summarization quality, by mitigating interference and enabling positive transfer through shared components.
proposals.method: We will design a modular adapter architecture atop a frozen multilingual LLM backbone (e.g., BLOOM or XGLM). Each language pair (L1->L2) will be served by a composition of a language-specific encoder adapter, a cross-lingual summarization task adapter, and a language-specific decoder adapter. We will explore efficient routing and composition strategies, including conditional computation and mixture-of-experts layers for the shared task module. The system will be trained and evaluated on a novel, extended version of a multilingual summarization dataset (inspired by XL-Sum), scaled to 50+ languages, with rigorous automatic and human evaluation for faithfulness and content preservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, parameter-efficient architecture blueprint for scalable multilingual NLP; 2) a comprehensive analysis of interference and transfer in massively multilingual PEFT setups; and 3) an extended, publicly released benchmark for evaluating multilingual summarization at scale, advancing the field towards truly global language coverage.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic compositional design and scaling of PEFT modules for summarization across a massively multilingual spectrum (50+ languages), moving beyond bilingual or few-language studies, and rigorously evaluating the trade-offs between parameter efficiency, task performance, and linguistic coverage.

proposals.title: Adversarial Language Invariance for Faithful Multilingual Summarization
proposals.motivation: Multilingual summarization models often learn language-specific features in their interlingua representations, which can lead to unfaithful or biased summaries that distort source content. This is a critical issue for real-world applications requiring consistent, high-quality information across languages. Existing methods for learning language-agnostic representations in multilingual models are not explicitly designed for the high-stakes task of summarization, where faithfulness is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a language-adversarial objective into the encoder of a multilingual summarization model will more effectively strip language-specific features from the interlingua, leading to summaries that are more faithful to the source content across languages.
proposals.method: We will build upon a transformer-based multilingual abstractive summarization architecture (e.g., mBART). A gradient reversal layer will be added between the encoder's output and a language classifier head. The model will be trained with a combined loss: the standard sequence-to-sequence summarization loss (e.g., cross-entropy) plus an adversarial loss that maximizes the language classifier's error, forcing the encoder to produce language-invariant representations. We will evaluate on established multilingual summarization datasets (e.g., MLSUM) and use faithfulness metrics from STORYSUMM, such as question-answering-based factuality checks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for improving faithfulness in multilingual summarization by directly optimizing for language-invariant content representation. It will produce a new, publicly available model and a detailed analysis of how adversarial invariance correlates with established faithfulness metrics, providing insights for future work on robust cross-lingual generation.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial domain adaptation is established in computer vision and some NLP tasks, its application to enforce language invariance specifically for the goal of improving faithfulness in summarization is novel. This approach moves beyond standard multilingual pre-training objectives to directly combat a root cause of cross-lingual unfaithfulness.

proposals.title: Controllable Paraphrase Generation via a Learned Interlingual Representation
proposals.motivation: Current paraphrase generation models often lack fine-grained control over the semantic and stylistic dimensions of variation, leading to outputs that are either too similar or unfaithful. A learned interlingua, as explored in multilingual neural machine translation pre-2026, offers a promising disentangled semantic representation. This project investigates its inverse use for controlled monolingual generation, addressing a core need in data augmentation, summarization, and reducing model bias through diverse rephrasing.
proposals.hypothesis: A semantic interlingua, trained via multilingual objectives, can be used as a controlled source to generate diverse, faithful paraphrases in a single language by systematically perturbing its latent dimensions.
proposals.method: We will first train a multilingual encoder-decoder model (e.g., mT5 or a custom Transformer) on parallel corpora to induce a language-agnostic interlingua in its encoder's latent space. We will then freeze the decoder and train a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) or a diffusion model to map from the original English sentence to its interlingual representation and back, introducing controlled noise. By sampling from the latent distribution around this point and decoding, we will generate diverse paraphrases. Faithfulness will be evaluated using metrics from STORYSUMM, and diversity via self-BLEU and lexical/ syntactic measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for controlled paraphrase generation with explicit semantic knobs. It will produce a new diagnostic dataset and evaluation protocol for assessing paraphrase diversity and faithfulness, extending themes from PERSONA and STORYSUMM. The resulting model can serve as a testbed for studying and mitigating stylistic biases in text generation.
proposals.novelty: While interlingua representations have been studied for translation, their systematic inversion for controlled monolingual generation is unexplored. This approach moves beyond typical sequence-to-sequence paraphrase models by leveraging a disentangled, semantically-grounded latent space for fine-grained control.

proposals.title: Scaling Interlingua Representations: A Study on Information Preservation and Zero-Shot Transfer for Summarization
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation and multilingual text generation models often use a compressed, language-agnostic 'interlingua' representation. Pre-2026 work suggests this bottleneck may discard nuanced, language-specific information crucial for high-quality generation tasks like summarization. Systematically studying the relationship between interlingua capacity and task performance is essential for building more faithful and effective cross-lingual systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling up the dimensionality and capacity of the interlingua representation in a multilingual encoder-decoder model will lead to measurable improvements in information preservation (faithfulness) and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance for abstractive summarization.
proposals.method: We will extend a multilingual sequence-to-sequence architecture (e.g., mT5 or mBART) by modifying its cross-attention bottleneck. We will create model variants with progressively larger interlingua vector dimensions (e.g., 128 to 2048) and introduce small adapter modules to increase its parametric capacity. Using a multi-document summarization dataset like MLSUM, we will train models to summarize into English from multiple source languages, then evaluate zero-shot performance on unseen language pairs. Faithfulness will be assessed using methods from STORYSUMM, such as question-answering and entailment-based metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of interlingua scaling for generation, establishing empirical scaling laws for cross-lingual summarization. It will contribute a new diagnostic framework and model variants that clarify the trade-offs between representation compression and task performance, directly informing the design of more robust multilingual NLP systems.
proposals.novelty: While scaling model parameters is common, targeted scaling of the specific interlingua subspace within a fixed-size model is unexplored. This approach isolates the effect of the representation bottleneck itself, moving beyond simple model size increases.

proposals.title: DensityPredict: Learning to Model the Human-Preferred Trade-off Between Informativeness and Readability in Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current automatic summarization evaluation focuses on isolated dimensions like ROUGE (informativeness) or linguistic quality scores, failing to capture the nuanced trade-off between these dimensions that human judges implicitly optimize for. This gap necessitates expensive human evaluation to determine the 'optimal density' for a summary. An automatic metric that predicts this human-preferred balance would significantly accelerate research and system development in controllable summarization.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned metric, trained on fine-grained human preference annotations for informativeness vs. readability, can accurately predict the summary density point most preferred by humans, outperforming the linear combination of existing isolated metrics.
proposals.method: We will first construct a dataset of summary pairs (or sets) from systems like BART, PEGASUS, and GPT-3.5, annotated via pairwise comparisons for overall preference with rationales tied to informativeness and readability. Using this dataset, we will train a regression model (e.g., a lightweight transformer or a model based on DeBERTa) to predict the human preference score. The model's input will be the source, candidate summary, and embeddings from pre-2026 models encoding established metrics (e.g., BERTScore for content, syntactic features for readability) as auxiliary features. We will evaluate against held-out human judgments and ablation studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dataset of human preferences on the density trade-off, 2) a new automatic evaluation metric, DensityPredict, that models this composite preference, and 3) an analysis of how the optimal density varies across domains (e.g., news vs. stories), advancing the field beyond single-dimension evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., STORYSUMM) evaluates faithfulness and PERSONA explores pluralistic alignment, no pre-2026 metric directly models the *trade-off* between core summary qualities as defined by human preference, moving from evaluating independent attributes to predicting their optimal synthesis.

proposals.title: METRICS-STRESS: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Stress-Testing Summarization Evaluation Metrics
proposals.motivation: Current summarization evaluation relies heavily on automated metrics like ROUGE, BERTScore, and newer LLM-based scorers, which are primarily validated by their correlation with human judgments on standard model outputs. This creates a blind spot: metrics are not systematically tested for their robustness to specific, challenging phenomena (e.g., factual contradictions, omission of salient entities, coherence breaks). As a result, progress in model development may be misdirected by flawed evaluation signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a benchmark with fine-grained, adversarially crafted summary variants will reveal significant and systematic failures in state-of-the-art evaluation metrics, uncorrelated with their performance on standard leaderboards.
proposals.method: We will create METRICS-STRESS, a benchmark built upon existing high-quality summarization datasets (e.g., XSum, CNN/DM). For a subset of source documents, we will generate 'perturbed' summaries via controlled transformations that introduce specific error types (e.g., entity swaps, temporal misordering, hallucinated details, incoherent splicing). Each perturbation will be annotated for error type and severity. The core evaluation will involve running a suite of metrics (lexical, neural, LLM-as-judge) on these perturbed summaries and analyzing their ability to detect the introduced errors, their correlation with human severity ratings, and their failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A publicly available diagnostic benchmark for stress-testing summarization metrics, shifting focus from model ranking to metric reliability. 2) A detailed analysis of metric vulnerabilities, guiding the development of more robust evaluation methods. 3) A framework for future metric evaluation that prioritizes granular error detection over aggregate scores.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Fabbri et al., 2021) has created faithfulness benchmarks for models, METRICS-STRESS inverts the focus to explicitly target the metrics themselves with adversarially constructed failures. It moves beyond correlation studies to causal, diagnostic testing of metric components.

proposals.title: Benchmark Co-Creation: Eliciting Novel Summarization Tasks via Model-Generated User Simulations
proposals.motivation: Static, human-authored benchmarks for summarization evaluation are inherently limited, reflecting known biases and failing to adapt to emergent model capabilities. This creates an evaluation gap where models are not assessed on tasks they can perform but humans have not yet conceived. A dynamic, model-informed benchmark creation process is needed to keep evaluation relevant and challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), when prompted to simulate diverse user personas and needs, can generate novel, valid, and high-quality summarization tasks that extend beyond the scope of existing human-authored benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PERSONA framework to generate a diverse set of simulated users with specific goals, expertise, and content preferences. Using a powerful LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Claude 3) as a simulator, we will prompt each persona to propose a detailed summarization task for a given source document, including instructions and evaluation criteria. We will then filter and validate these proposed tasks using a multi-stage process: automated checks for coherence, human assessment for novelty and clarity, and finally, testing the tasks on a suite of summarization models to confirm they are non-trivial and discriminative.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for dynamic benchmark creation and a new dataset of model-proposed summarization tasks. It will provide insights into the gap between human and model conceptualizations of summarization, offering a more rigorous and forward-looking evaluation paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses models to *perform* or *evaluate* tasks, we invert the paradigm by using models as *proposers* of the tasks themselves. This moves beyond identifying model biases to actively leveraging model capabilities for benchmark design.

proposals.title: GEM-Eval: A Systematic Benchmark for Novel Faithfulness and Bias Metrics in Summarization
proposals.motivation: Automated evaluation of text generation, especially summarization, remains a critical unsolved problem. Pre-2026, standard metrics like ROUGE often fail to capture semantic faithfulness and subtle biases, while human evaluation is costly and non-reproducible. The GEM benchmark provides a standardized, multi-task environment ideal for systematically testing new evaluation strategies, but its potential for rigorous metric validation is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic testing within GEM will reveal that novel fine-grained metrics, combining LLM-based faithfulness scoring and bias detection frameworks, correlate more strongly with targeted human judgments than traditional lexical overlap metrics, and that their performance varies significantly across languages and domains.
proposals.method: We will implement and test a suite of novel automated metrics within the GEM infrastructure. This includes: (1) fine-grained, decompositional faithfulness metrics inspired by recent work like QAFactEval and the granular supervision in STORYSUMM; (2) bias detection methods adapted from PERSONA's pluralistic alignment testbed to measure demographic and perspective biases in summaries. We will conduct large-scale correlation studies against newly collected human annotations for faithfulness and bias on selected GEM tasks (e.g., Wikilingua, MLSum), controlling for language and domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a public benchmark suite within GEM for evaluating new summarization metrics, providing standardized datasets and human judgments. It will deliver a rigorous analysis of where current state-of-the-art automated metrics succeed and fail, offering concrete guidance for future metric development and more reliable model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While GEM is used for model benchmarking, this proposal repurposes it as a rigorous testbed for evaluation metrics themselves. The integration of fine-grained faithfulness scoring with pluralistic bias evaluation into a unified, multi-lingual benchmark represents a novel, holistic approach to assessing summary quality.

proposals.title: PsychoSumm: A Psychometric Framework for Validating and Benchmarking Summarization Evaluation Metrics
proposals.motivation: Current automatic metrics for text summarization (e.g., ROUGE, BERTScore, QAEval) are widely used but poorly understood in terms of their measurement properties. Their validity (do they measure what they claim?) and reliability (do they produce consistent scores?) are rarely formally assessed, leading to potential misalignment with human judgments and unreliable benchmarking. This gap mirrors the pre-2026 need for more rigorous, human-centric evaluation in NLP, as highlighted by work on faithfulness and pluralistic alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying formal psychometric testing—specifically, assessing construct validity, inter-rater reliability, and test-retest reliability—to NLG evaluation metrics will reveal systematic biases and limitations not captured by simple correlation with human ratings, enabling the creation of more robust and interpretable metric benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will adapt methods from educational and psychological testing. First, we will operationalize 'construct validity' by designing controlled experiments on existing datasets (e.g., SummEval, REALSumm, STORYSUMM) to test if metrics are sensitive to specific textual constructs (e.g., factual consistency, coherence) versus irrelevant factors (e.g., length, lexical overlap). We will use multi-trait multi-method (MTMM) matrices and factor analysis. Second, we will assess 'reliability' by measuring metric consistency across semantically equivalent summary variations (parallel-forms reliability) and across model-generated text distributions (generalizability theory). This framework will be implemented as an open-source toolkit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, psychometrically-grounded framework for diagnosing the strengths and failure modes of summarization metrics. It will yield a new benchmark, PsychoSumm, that scores metrics on validity and reliability dimensions, moving beyond aggregate correlation. This contributes directly to more trustworthy and transparent evaluation in line with the researcher's focus on human-centered assessment and dataset creation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques metrics via correlation studies, this proposal formally adapts established psychometric theory—a cornerstone of measurement in social sciences—to NLG evaluation, treating automatic metrics as 'raters' whose measurement properties must be rigorously validated. This approach is novel for summarization and moves evaluation from a 'best correlation wins' paradigm to a diagnostic one.

proposals.title: CURRI-LM: Data-Efficient Pretraining via Synthesized Curricula and Selective Exposure
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) rely on massive, undifferentiated web-scale corpora, raising immense computational, environmental, and data-quality concerns. This paradigm neglects established principles from cognitive science, where structured curricula are demonstrably more efficient for learning than unstructured exposure. This research challenges the necessity of sheer data scale by investigating whether intelligently sequenced training data can yield superior or comparable model performance with orders-of-magnitude less compute.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model trained on a significantly smaller, algorithmically synthesized curriculum of tokens—ordered by linguistic and reasoning complexity with strategic repetition—will achieve Pareto improvements, matching or surpassing the performance of models trained on vastly larger, random corpora across key capabilities.
proposals.method: We will develop a curriculum synthesis algorithm that, given a large raw corpus (e.g., C4, The Pile), generates a structured training sequence. This involves: 1) defining a multi-dimensional complexity metric (lexical, syntactic, discourse, reasoning) for text chunks, 2) using fine-grained supervision (inspired by Ladhak et al.'s alignment work) to train a curriculum planner model, and 3) implementing a selective exposure trainer that schedules data based on the curriculum and model-progress feedback. We will train decoder-only LMs (e.g., Pythia-scale) on our 100B-token curriculum and compare them to baselines trained on random subsets and full datasets, evaluating on diverse benchmarks (e.g., summarization via STORYSUMM, MMLU, BIG-bench).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient pretraining, directly challenging the 'more data is always better' assumption. It will yield a publicly released methodology for curriculum synthesis and empirical evidence on the sufficiency of smaller, high-quality datasets, potentially reducing the resource barrier for LLM development. The evaluation will extend to assessing biases and faithfulness, linking curriculum design to model trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning exists in narrow domains, applying it at the scale and abstraction of foundational LM pretraining is novel. The core innovation is the automated synthesis of a global pretraining curriculum from raw text, moving beyond simple filtering or heuristics to a learned, dynamic sequencing strategy.

proposals.title: RIDeR: Training a Retrieval-Infused Decoder for Robust Post-Retrieval Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems often fail when the retrieved evidence is noisy, contradictory, or incomplete, leading to unfaithful or ungrounded outputs. This is a critical bottleneck for trustworthy applications like summarization and question answering. Current methods largely treat the retriever and generator as separate components, with limited training to specialize the generator for the challenging synthesis task after retrieval.
proposals.hypothesis: A decoder-only language model, trained end-to-end with a frozen bi-encoder retriever and a modified attention mechanism to explicitly attend to a structured block of retrieved representations, can be specialized to perform more robust evidence synthesis and verification from imperfect retrieval sets than standard RAG fine-tuning approaches.
proposals.method: We will augment a standard decoder LM (e.g., T5 or a small GPT variant) with a frozen, off-the-shelf bi-encoder (e.g., Contriever or DPR). For each query, the retriever provides a fixed set of passage embeddings. We will modify the decoder's cross-attention (or add a dedicated attention head) to treat these retrieved embeddings as a contiguous, structured context block, separate from the original query. The model will be trained end-to-end with a language modeling objective on tasks like question answering or claim verification, where the gold answer must be synthesized from the provided, often adversarial or noisy, retrieved evidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel hybrid architecture and training paradigm for post-retrieval reasoning, a specialized model checkpoint, and a new evaluation protocol measuring robustness to retrieval noise. It directly contributes to the field of faithful text generation and model alignment by grounding synthesis in provided evidence.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior RAG work that jointly fine-tunes the retriever, we freeze it to force the decoder to adapt to its fixed, imperfect behavior. The explicit architectural modification to attend to a structured retrieval block is distinct from simply concatenating text, aiming to teach the model to reason *about* the retrieved set.

proposals.title: EcoLingua: A Biodiversity-Inspired Framework for Mapping the Performance Landscape of Multilingual Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual model evaluation often relies on aggregated scores from a few high-resource languages, masking systematic performance disparities. This fails to reveal whether models are true generalists or have weaknesses correlated with specific linguistic features or data scarcity. A principled, stratified evaluation framework is needed to diagnose model health across the full spectrum of language diversity, analogous to how biodiversity surveys assess ecosystem robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a biodiversity survey framework—systematically sampling across gradients of language family, typology, and resource level—will reveal that ModernBERT's performance exhibits significant 'biodiversity loss' in low-resource and typologically distant languages, and that performance 'richness' and 'evenness' are predictable from linguistic and data features.
proposals.method: We will construct a stratified evaluation 'transect' by curating and adapting existing benchmarks (e.g., XTREME, XGLUE, TyDi QA) across a matrix of language families, typological features (from WALS), and resource tiers. For each sampled 'habitat' (e.g., high-resource fusional vs. low-resource agglutinative), we will compute ecological metrics: performance 'species richness' (number of tasks where accuracy exceeds a baseline), 'evenness' (variance across tasks), and 'functional diversity' (performance spread across different task types). We will use ModernBERT as our focal 'species' and perform correlation and regression analyses to link performance metrics to environmental gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, interpretable 'performance biodiversity' map for a state-of-the-art multilingual model, moving beyond average scores to a diagnostic tool. It will provide concrete guidance for more equitable model development by identifying the linguistic and resource dimensions where performance collapses. We will release the stratified benchmark design and analysis code as a reproducible testbed for future model audits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied performance gaps, the explicit adaptation of ecological biodiversity metrics (richness, evenness, functional diversity) and sampling strategies to model evaluation is novel. This frames the problem not as benchmarking but as ecosystem assessment, offering a new conceptual and methodological lens from computational sustainability.

proposals.title: Noise-Robust Post-Training Compression for Efficient and Faithful Multilingual Models
proposals.motivation: Deploying large multilingual models like ModernBERT is costly, and standard post-training compression (pruning, quantization) can degrade performance, especially on lower-resource languages. This degradation is often exacerbated by the inherent noise and imperfect alignments in the cross-lingual data used for pre-training. We need compression methods that are robust to this noise to create efficient, reliable models for global applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the noise and misalignments in a model's cross-lingual representations during the compression process will yield a smaller model that is not only efficient but also more robust and faithful in its multilingual outputs compared to standard compression applied to a 'cleaned' model.
proposals.method: We will adapt iterative magnitude pruning and quantization-aware training (QAT) for a compressed ModernBERT. The key innovation is the training signal: instead of a standard loss, we will use a noise-aware objective. During compression fine-tuning, we will inject synthetic noise (e.g., random token swaps, synonym replacement) into the input or intermediate representations, simulating cross-lingual misalignments, and train the model to maintain stable semantic outputs. Evaluation will leverage multilingual summarization and NLI benchmarks, with a focus on faithfulness metrics inspired by STORYSUMM.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel post-training compression framework that leverages, rather than avoids, cross-lingual noise. 2) A highly compressed, deployable multilingual model with demonstrated robustness on noisy, real-world tasks. 3) New insights into the relationship between representation alignment noise and model compression stability.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard pipeline: instead of de-noising then compressing, we use the compression phase to actively train for noise robustness, creating a synergy where compression enhances cross-lingual reliability—a goal neither approach achieves independently.

proposals.title: DEBIAS-GEN: Generating Counter-Stereotypical Narratives via Text-to-Image Models for Debiasing Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) and text-to-image models often perpetuate and amplify harmful societal biases learned from their training data. Existing debiasing methods, such as data filtering or fine-tuning on handcrafted counter-examples, are limited in scale and diversity. This project addresses the need for scalable, high-quality, and diverse counter-stereotypical data to effectively retrain models and mitigate these ingrained biases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that text-to-image models, guided by structured prompts, can generate a diverse and high-quality dataset of counter-stereotypical visual-textual pairs, and that using this data for fine-tuning will measurably reduce stereotype bias in downstream language models without degrading their core capabilities.
proposals.method: We will first curate a seed set of stereotypical associations (e.g., 'nurse' -> 'female') from pre-2026 bias benchmarks. Using a state-of-the-art text-to-image model (e.g., Stable Diffusion), we will generate counter-stereotypical images via carefully engineered prompts. A vision-language model (e.g., BLIP-2) will then caption these images to create descriptive, model-generated textual narratives. This synthetic (image, counter-narrative) dataset will be used to fine-tune a target LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2) via instruction-tuning, explicitly teaching it to generate descriptions that counter stereotypes. We will evaluate the debiased model on established benchmarks like StereoSet and CrowS-Pairs, and assess its faithfulness and quality on summarization tasks using adapted metrics from STORYSUMM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, scalable pipeline for generating counter-stereotypical training data, 2) a new multimodal dataset for debiasing research, and 3) an empirical evaluation of using synthetic visual data to mitigate textual bias in LLMs, advancing methods for pluralistic alignment as explored in PERSONA.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human-written or filtered data for debiasing, our approach uniquely inverts the typical data creation pipeline by leveraging generative image models as a source of counter-stereotypical concepts, creating a novel synergy between multimodal generation and language model alignment.

proposals.title: PsychoSum: A Psychometric Framework for Reliable and Valid Human Evaluation of Text Summaries
proposals.motivation: Human evaluation remains the gold standard for assessing summary quality, but protocols are often ad-hoc, leading to low inter-annotator agreement and poor construct validity. This unreliability hinders reproducible research and the development of robust automatic metrics. Grounding evaluation in established psychometric theory, which has a century of history in measuring complex human traits, offers a principled solution to these persistent challenges in NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying classical test theory and item response theory (IRT) to the design of evaluation rubrics and the analysis of rater judgments, we can create human evaluation protocols with significantly higher reliability (inter-rater agreement) and construct validity for dimensions like faithfulness and coherence.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-dimensional evaluation instrument (e.g., for faithfulness, coherence, relevance) using Likert-scale items informed by prior work like STORYSUMM. We will collect large-scale annotations on diverse summarization datasets. Using psychometric packages (e.g., `lme4`, `mirt` in R), we will perform reliability analysis (Cronbach's alpha, G-coefficients) and IRT modeling to identify poorly performing items or raters, iteratively refine the rubric, and calibrate rater severity/leniency. The final output is a calibrated evaluation protocol and a statistical model for adjusting raw scores.
proposals.contribution: 1) A publicly available, psychometrically validated evaluation toolkit for the summarization community. 2) A dataset of calibrated human judgments with modeled rater biases, enabling fairer model comparison. 3) Demonstrated guidelines for designing more reliable human evaluations in NLP, moving beyond simple majority vote or average scores.
proposals.novelty: While psychometrics is standard in social sciences, its systematic application to NLP evaluation is nascent. This work moves beyond using correlation with averages as a validity measure, instead using full psychometric modeling to diagnose and improve the evaluation instrument itself, akin to establishing a 'standardized test' for summaries.

proposals.title: ParaScore: Quantifying Paraphrastic Divergence in Summarization via Style Transfer Metrics
proposals.motivation: Current automatic summarization evaluation focuses heavily on content overlap (e.g., ROUGE) and factual consistency, but lacks robust metrics for assessing the degree and quality of paraphrasing—the rewording of source content. This is a critical gap, as high-quality summarization often requires sophisticated paraphrasing to improve fluency and conciseness, yet excessive or poor paraphrasing can introduce unfaithfulness. Pre-2026, the text style transfer (TST) field has developed metrics to quantify attribute change and content preservation when altering text style (e.g., sentiment, formality), offering a promising but unexplored framework for paraphrase analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metrics adapted from text style transfer, which jointly model attribute change and content preservation, can effectively quantify the amount and linguistic quality of paraphrasing in summaries, correlating better with human judgments of paraphrase adequacy and fluency than existing surface-level metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt and unify established TST evaluation dimensions: 'Paraphrastic Strength' (quantifying lexical/syntactic divergence using metrics like BLEURT or BERTScore for content preservation and edit distance for surface change), 'Fluency' (using pre-2026 language model perplexity), and 'Semantic Preservation' (using NLI models or sentence embeddings). We will construct a controlled benchmark by perturbing human-written summaries from datasets like CNN/DM or XSum to create graded levels of paraphrasing. We will then validate our composite metric, ParaScore, against human annotations of paraphrase quality collected via a structured protocol, and analyze its correlation with existing faithfulness metrics from work like STORYSUMM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for quantifying paraphrasing in summarization, moving beyond binary faithfulness checks. It will deliver a new diagnostic tool for analyzing summarization model biases (e.g., over-reliance on copying vs. incoherent rewriting) and a reproducible testbed for evaluating paraphrase generation, similar in spirit to the PERSONA testbed for pluralistic alignment.
proposals.novelty: While style transfer metrics exist, their application to decompose and measure paraphrasing—a core but nebulous summarization sub-task—is novel. This approach reframes paraphrase analysis not as a binary task but as a continuous, multi-dimensional evaluation problem.

proposals.title: When Does the Loop Close? Establishing the Sufficiency of Automated Metrics for Summarization Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Human evaluation is the gold standard for assessing text generation tasks like summarization, but it is costly, slow, and difficult to scale, hindering rapid model development and dataset creation. While automated metrics (e.g., ROUGE, BERTScore, LLM-as-a-judge) are efficient, their alignment with human judgments is inconsistent and task-dependent, leading to a persistent need for human-in-the-loop validation. This work aims to systematically determine the conditions under which automated metrics can reliably substitute for human evaluation, thereby reducing research costs and accelerating iteration cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for specific, well-defined sub-tasks of summarization (e.g., factual consistency in news summarization), a carefully calibrated ensemble of modern neural metrics can achieve a level of agreement with human judgments that renders additional interactive human evaluation statistically unnecessary for model comparison.
proposals.method: We will construct a meta-dataset of human evaluation results across diverse summarization tasks (e.g., from STORYSUMM, SummEval) and correlate them with a suite of automated metrics, including reference-based (ROUGE, BLEURT), reference-free (BERTScore, BARTScore), and LLM-based evaluators (using models like GPT-4). We will train a diagnostic classifier (e.g., a logistic regression or small transformer) on task metadata (domain, summary length, model type) and metric scores to predict when human-machine agreement will be high. The core analysis will establish statistical confidence boundaries for replacing human scores with metric ensembles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, evidence-based framework for researchers to decide when to deploy automated evaluation confidently, saving resources. It will contribute a public diagnostic tool and guidelines for the summarization community, extending the principles of reproducible evaluation from testbeds like PERSONA to the evaluation methodology itself.
proposals.novelty: Instead of proposing a new metric, we invert the problem to diagnostically predict the reliability of existing metrics per task, moving beyond reporting correlations to prescribing evaluation protocols. This directly addresses the cost-benefit trade-off inherent in human-in-the-loop evaluation, a core concern in scalable NLP.

proposals.title: Decoupling Faithfulness from Extractiveness: A Framework for Abstractive Summarization with Verifiable Content Grounding
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models often generate unfaithful hallucinations, while faithfulness evaluation metrics (e.g., QAGS, SummaC, FactCC) frequently correlate strongly with extractive content overlap. This creates a false dichotomy, limiting the field's ability to produce novel, abstractive summaries that are verifiably faithful. Addressing this is crucial for deploying trustworthy summarization in high-stakes domains like news, science, and medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: Faithfulness in summarization is not inherently tied to lexical or syntactic overlap with the source. By explicitly modeling and verifying content-level entailment and grounding, we can develop abstractive summarization systems that achieve high faithfulness scores without relying on extractive copying.
proposals.method: We will construct a new dataset of abstractive, human-written summaries annotated for fine-grained content units and their source provenance, building on methods from STORYSUMM. We will then develop a two-stage model: 1) A content planning module (using fine-grained supervision akin to Ladhak et al.'s alignment work) to generate a structured content plan of verifiable propositions. 2) A constrained generation module that realizes the plan into fluent text, with its output monitored by a learned faithfulness discriminator trained to detect novel, unsupported content. Evaluation will use both automated metrics (e.g., improved versions of QAEval) and human studies for faithfulness, abstractiveness, and quality.
proposals.contribution: 1) A new dataset and benchmark for abstractive, faithful summarization. 2) A novel model architecture that decouples content planning from surface realization to enhance faithfulness. 3) New insights into the relationship between abstractiveness and faithfulness, challenging a core assumption in summarization evaluation.
proposals.novelty: This work directly challenges the prevailing assumption that faithfulness requires extractiveness, a bias evident in many datasets and metrics. It proposes a new paradigm where faithfulness is enforced at the content planning stage, enabling truly abstractive yet verifiable generation.

proposals.title: Generalizing Learned Selectors: A Framework for Dataset- and Model-Agnostic Summary Quality Control
proposals.motivation: Recent work on learned selectors, which choose the best summary from multiple candidates, has shown promise for improving summarization quality and mitigating model biases. However, these selectors are typically trained and evaluated on narrow domains (e.g., news, stories) and a limited set of model architectures, raising concerns about their robustness and generalizability. A systematic investigation into their performance across diverse data distributions and modern summarization models is needed to establish them as a reliable component in real-world NLP pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a selector framework, trained with fine-grained supervision on a strategically constructed multi-dataset corpus, can generalize effectively to unseen datasets and summarization model architectures, consistently outperforming single-model baselines and heuristic selection methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the fine-grained supervision framework from 'Aligning Large Language Models via Fine-grained Supervision' to train a unified selector model. We will generate candidate summaries using a diverse set of pre-2026 architectures (e.g., BART, PEGASUS, T5, and instruction-tuned LLMs like Flan-T5) on multiple datasets spanning domains like dialogue (SAMSum), scientific papers (PubMed), and long narratives (from STORYSUMM). The selector will be trained to predict fine-grained quality scores (e.g., for faithfulness, coherence, relevance) using a multi-task objective, and its generalization will be tested on held-out datasets and models not seen during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of selector generalization, yielding practical insights for deploying summary selection in diverse applications. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating summary selectors and a robust, pre-trained selector model to facilitate future research and application in controllable text generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work builds selectors for specific model-dataset pairs, this proposal systematically investigates cross-domain and cross-architectural generalization, a critical step towards a truly model-agnostic quality control module for summarization.

proposals.title: Beyond Summarization: A Unified Trade-off Curve Framework for Evaluating Faithfulness vs. Divergence in Text Generation
proposals.motivation: The faithfulness-abstractiveness trade-off curve, introduced for summarization (Kryscinski et al., 2020), provides a nuanced, system-level view of a model's performance frontier. However, similar tensions between fidelity to source content and creative or task-appropriate divergence exist in other generative tasks like dialogue (where responses must be relevant to context) and data-to-text (where texts must accurately reflect structured inputs). Pre-2026, evaluations for these tasks often treat faithfulness and quality metrics in isolation, lacking a unified framework to analyze their inherent trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: The trade-off curve framework is generalizable beyond summarization. For tasks like dialogue and data-to-text, systematically varying model parameters (e.g., sampling temperature, decoding constraints) will reveal a Pareto frontier between faithfulness and appropriate divergence, and the shape of this curve will be a strong indicator of overall model capability and failure modes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the trade-off curve methodology to two new domains: open-domain dialogue (using datasets like DailyDialog) and data-to-text (using WebNLG). For each task, we will define a task-specific faithfulness metric (e.g., next utterance relevance score for dialogue, relation generation accuracy for data-to-text) and a divergence metric (e.g., lexical novelty, syntactic diversity). We will generate outputs from state-of-the-art models (e.g., fine-tuned T5, GPT-2/3) across a spectrum of decoding strategies, including nucleus sampling with varying p, beam search with length penalty, and constrained decoding. The resulting curves will be analyzed to compare model families and training paradigms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, cross-task analysis of the faithfulness-divergence trade-off, yielding a new diagnostic tool for model developers. We will release task-specific evaluation suites and benchmarks, enabling more nuanced comparisons of generative models. The findings will inform the design of training objectives and decoding methods that better navigate this fundamental tension.
proposals.novelty: While the trade-off is known, applying the formal curve analysis framework to dialogue and data-to-text is novel. It moves beyond task-specific metrics to propose a general evaluation paradigm for any conditional generation task where output must balance source fidelity with generative freedom.

proposals.title: FAITHSPAN: A Spectrum-Aware Framework for Intrinsic Faithfulness Evaluation in Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current automatic metrics for summary faithfulness, such as fact-level entailment or question-answering (QA) based methods, often fail to generalize across the abstractiveness spectrum. They perform well on extractive summaries but degrade for highly abstractive ones, where the semantic gap between source and summary is larger. This limits their utility for evaluating modern LLM-generated summaries and hinders progress in developing more faithful summarization models.
proposals.hypothesis: A faithfulness metric that explicitly models and adapts to the degree of abstractiveness in a summary will achieve significantly higher correlation with human judgments across diverse summarization datasets and models.
proposals.method: We will develop FAITHSPAN, a multi-stage framework. First, we will train a lightweight regressor to predict the abstractiveness level of a summary relative to its source, using features like n-gram overlap and semantic similarity. Based on this predicted level, FAITHSPAN will dynamically weight or combine evidence from complementary intrinsic evaluation modules: a fine-grained fact extraction and entailment module (effective for extractive content) and a latent variable model that learns to map abstractive summaries to their likely source propositions. We will train and validate the framework on a composite dataset built from existing faithfulness benchmarks (e.g., SummEval, QAGS, the researcher's own STORYSUMM) and evaluate on held-out datasets spanning news, dialogue, and story domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more robust automatic metric for faithfulness evaluation that works reliably across different types of summaries. It will also contribute a novel, publicly available composite benchmark for training and testing faithfulness metrics, advancing reproducible research in summarization evaluation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior metrics that apply a one-size-fits-all approach, FAITHSPAN is the first to explicitly condition its verification mechanism on the predicted abstractiveness of the summary, creating a spectrum-aware evaluation paradigm. This bridges the gap between extractive-oriented fact-checking and the challenges of verifying highly abstractive text.

proposals.title: Sentence-Level Compression as a Testbed for Faithfulness and Bias in Abstractive Summarization
proposals.motivation: Current abstractive summarization models, especially large language models (LLMs), often exhibit factual inconsistencies and subtle biases, but these errors are difficult to isolate and analyze in complex, multi-document summaries. A simpler, sentence-level compression task provides a controlled environment to study the core generation challenges of faithfulness and content selection without the confounding complexity of discourse-level reasoning. This aligns with the need for more granular evaluation benchmarks, as highlighted by works like STORYSUMM, to diagnose and improve model reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fine-tuning LLMs on a high-quality, sentence-level compression dataset with fine-grained supervision on content units, we can achieve higher and more measurable gains in faithfulness and controllability than is typically observed in full-document summarization, revealing fundamental bottlenecks in content preservation.
proposals.method: We will first construct a novel dataset of sentence-compression pairs, annotated with fine-grained alignments between source content units (e.g., entities, relations, modifiers) and their compressed counterparts, extending methodologies from granular supervision research. We will then fine-tune a pre-2026 LLM (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only model like LLaMA) using a multi-task objective that combines standard sequence generation with auxiliary losses on the aligned content units. Evaluation will leverage both automated metrics (e.g., factuality scores using question-answering) and a human assessment framework inspired by PERSONA to measure faithfulness, bias, and compression quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new diagnostic dataset and benchmark for sentence-level compression with explicit faithfulness annotations, 2) empirical evidence on the limits and capabilities of LLMs in preserving factual content under strict compression, and 3) insights into whether improvements at the sentence level translate to more faithful long-form summarization, informing future model and evaluation design.
proposals.novelty: While sentence compression is a known task, the novelty lies in framing it explicitly as a controlled testbed for isolating and quantifying faithfulness and bias issues in abstractive LLMs, using fine-grained supervision techniques typically applied to larger-scale alignment problems.

proposals.title: Inverse Benchmarking: A Data Generation Framework for Contamination-Free Evaluation of Summarization Models
proposals.motivation: The widespread contamination of LLM training data with public benchmarks, such as those for summarization, critically undermines evaluation integrity. This leads to inflated performance metrics that do not reflect true model capability or generalization, a major concern in pre-2026 model assessment. A method to generate novel, uncontaminated evaluation datasets is urgently needed to restore trust in empirical results.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the logic of an existing evaluation test—specifically, using its scoring criteria as a generative constraint—we can algorithmically produce novel, high-quality evaluation instances that are semantically and structurally aligned with the original benchmark's goals but are guaranteed to be absent from training corpora.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that takes a summarization benchmark's scoring rubric (e.g., for faithfulness from STORYSUMM or for persona alignment from PERSONA) and formalizes it as a set of constraints for a controlled text generation model. Using fine-grained supervision techniques from prior alignment work, we will train a conditional language model (e.g., based on T5 or GPT-2 architectures) to generate source story-document pairs that satisfy specific rubric criteria. A filtering pipeline, using n-gram and neural embedding similarity against known training data (e.g., the Pile, C4), will verify novelty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel methodology for creating uncontaminated evaluation datasets, 2) a new, publicly released benchmark for summarization faithfulness and control, and 3) a revised performance ranking of contemporary LLMs on a truly held-out test set, revealing potential overestimation in prior evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on detecting contamination or creating new datasets via crowd-sourcing, our approach is the first to algorithmically invert an evaluation test's logic for systematic, scalable, and controllable generation of guaranteed-novel test items.

proposals.title: The Static-Dynamic Gap: Benchmarking the Robustness of Interactive Summarization Models to User-Driven Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: Modern LLMs are increasingly deployed in interactive settings where user feedback shapes model outputs and subsequent learning. However, their evaluation predominantly relies on static benchmarks (e.g., CNN/Daily Mail for summarization). This creates a critical gap: a model may excel on a static test set but degrade unpredictably when adapting to dynamic user preferences and interaction patterns, a scenario not captured by current evaluation paradigms. This problem is acute for tasks like summarization, where user-defined notions of quality (e.g., persona-based preferences, highlighted in PERSONA) can shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of summarization models fine-tuned via user interaction feedback will show significant and unpredictable divergence from their static benchmark scores, revealing a 'static-dynamic gap' that current benchmarks fail to measure.
proposals.method: We will first construct a novel testbed, building on the PERSONA and STORYSUMM frameworks, to simulate dynamic user interactions. We will fine-tune a base summarization model (e.g., T5, BART) using reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or fine-grained supervised learning (as in Ladhak's alignment work) on this interactive stream. Crucially, we will periodically evaluate the evolving model not only on the dynamic stream but also on a held-out static test set. We will analyze performance trajectories, measuring divergence (e.g., via performance correlation decay, emergence of new error modes) and link shifts to specific interaction types (e.g., preference for conciseness, factual emphasis).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a methodological framework and metrics for quantifying the static-dynamic performance gap in interactive NLP systems, 2) empirical evidence challenging the sufficiency of static benchmarks for models that learn from users, and 3) a new publicly released testbed for evaluating model robustness to user-driven distribution shift, extending the reproducibility goals of PERSONA.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied dataset biases and static evaluation limits (e.g., STORYSUMM on faithfulness), this proposal directly targets the core assumption of benchmark validity for *interactive, learning* systems. It moves beyond analyzing static data artifacts to actively engineering and measuring distribution shift induced by simulated user interaction loops.

proposals.title: Beyond English: A Framework for Diagnosing and Mitigating Cultural and Linguistic Biases in Summarization Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The evaluation of large language models (LLMs) is overwhelmingly English-centric, relying on benchmarks, metrics, and human evaluators primarily from English-speaking contexts. This creates a critical blind spot, as models optimized for English may fail to capture cultural nuances, discourse structures, and factual correctness in other languages, limiting their global utility and fairness. Pre-2026 work on summarization evaluation, like STORYSUMM, has focused on faithfulness but largely within a monolingual or English paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current English-centric evaluation protocols systematically underestimate performance gaps and introduce cultural biases when assessing LLMs for summarization in non-English languages and for content from non-Western cultural contexts.
proposals.method: We will construct SUMMCULTURE, a novel multilingual, multi-cultural summarization diagnostic dataset. Building on methods from PERSONA and STORYSUMM, we will source parallel news articles and narratives in English, Arabic, Mandarin, and Hindi, annotated for culturally salient entities and narrative structures. We will evaluate state-of-the-art multilingual LLMs using both automated metrics (e.g., BERTScore, QA-based faithfulness) and fine-grained human evaluation via a pluralistic annotator pool. The core analysis will correlate performance drops with specific linguistic features (e.g., morphology, discourse markers) and cultural references, using statistical models to isolate bias sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A diagnostic framework and dataset (SUMMCULTURE) to audit cultural and linguistic biases in summarization evaluation. 2) Empirical evidence quantifying the limitations of English-centric evaluation for global LLMs. 3) Guidelines for constructing more equitable, pluralistic evaluation protocols, advancing the field towards truly multilingual and culturally-aware model assessment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created multilingual summarization datasets, our approach uniquely integrates fine-grained cultural annotation and a diagnostic evaluation framework to explicitly dissect and attribute performance gaps, moving beyond mere translation-based evaluation to address deeper cultural alignment issues.

proposals.title: HELM-SUMM: Expanding Holistic Evaluation of Language Models with User-Centric Summarization Scenarios and Faithfulness Metrics
proposals.motivation: The Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) benchmark provides a crucial, standardized framework for assessing LM capabilities and risks. However, as of 2025, its coverage of text summarization remains limited, focusing primarily on generic news and dialogue summarization. This gap is significant because summarization is a high-impact, real-world application where failures in faithfulness (hallucination) and user-alignment (e.g., persona or intent adaptation) pose serious risks. A systematic expansion of HELM's summarization scenarios is needed to evaluate these nuanced capabilities and failures.
proposals.hypothesis: By augmenting the HELM benchmark with novel, user-centric summarization scenarios and corresponding fine-grained faithfulness metrics, we will reveal significant, previously unmeasured performance gaps and risk profiles in state-of-the-art language models that are not captured by existing generic summarization evaluations.
proposals.method: We will design and integrate two new core summarization scenarios into HELM. First, a 'Pluralistic Summarization' scenario, building on PERSONA, where models must generate summaries tailored to different reader personas (e.g., a domain expert vs. a novice), with metrics for persona adherence and bias. Second, a 'Complex Narrative Summarization' scenario, extending STORYSUMM, using long-form narratives with intricate plots and entities, evaluated with robust, fine-grained factual consistency metrics (e.g., using question-answering or entity-chain verification). We will implement these scenarios using the HELM framework, run evaluations on a suite of representative LMs (e.g, GPT-4, Claude, Llama), and analyze the results through a multi-dimensional lens of accuracy, faithfulness, and user alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a significant, publicly released expansion of the HELM benchmark with new summarization scenarios and metrics, 2) a comprehensive empirical analysis of modern LMs on these critical, user-aligned tasks, and 3) a methodological blueprint for integrating nuanced, application-focused evaluations into holistic benchmarking frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While HELM exists and summarization datasets are plentiful, the novelty lies in the systematic integration of user-centric, faithfulness-focused summarization tasks into a major holistic benchmark, moving beyond generic summary quality to evaluate alignment with specific user needs and the mitigation of context-specific hallucination risks.

proposals.title: HELM-TRACK: A Longitudinal Benchmark for Tracking Capabilities and Biases in Evolving Language Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid, continuous release of new and updated large language models (LLMs) creates a moving target for evaluation. Pre-2026, benchmarks like HELM provide a crucial standardized snapshot, but lack a systematic framework for longitudinal tracking. This makes it difficult to understand if model improvements are consistent across tasks, or if progress in one area (e.g., summarization) introduces new regressions in others (e.g., faithfulness or bias).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, longitudinal evaluation within the HELM framework will reveal non-uniform progress across model versions, identifying specific capability trade-offs and emergent biases that are obscured by one-off benchmarking.
proposals.method: We will extend the HELM framework to create a continuous evaluation pipeline. For each new or updated model (e.g., GPT-4 Turbo, Claude iterations, open-source Llama/Mistral variants), we will execute the core HELM scenarios, with a focused deep-dive on summarization tasks informed by STORYSUMM for faithfulness. We will employ fine-grained analysis methods from PERSONA to track pluralistic alignment and bias shifts across demographics. The pipeline will automate scoring, version pairing, and drift visualization.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce 1) a public, regularly updated leaderboard tracking model evolution, 2) a dataset of longitudinal model performance paired with version metadata, and 3) analysis identifying specific patterns of capability trade-offs and bias emergence. This provides the community with a diagnostic tool for model development and a clearer picture of holistic progress.
proposals.novelty: While HELM is a static benchmark, our proposal introduces a *longitudinal* and *continuous* evaluation paradigm. The novelty lies in systematically pairing model versioning data with multi-dimensional performance to analyze trends and trade-offs over time, moving beyond point-in-time comparisons.

proposals.title: MM-EvalSum: A Unified Framework for Faithfulness and Bias Evaluation in Multimodal Summarization
proposals.motivation: The rapid advancement of multimodal models (e.g., vision-language models) capable of summarizing complex inputs (e.g., videos, infographics) has outpaced the development of standardized evaluation. Pre-2026, text-only summarization evaluation has benefited from frameworks like STORYSUMM for faithfulness and PERSONA for pluralistic alignment, but no equivalent exists for multimodal outputs. This gap makes it difficult to systematically assess critical issues like hallucination of visual content or demographic biases in cross-modal generation, hindering reproducible progress and responsible deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a holistic, human-centric evaluation framework, extending principles from text-based summarization to multimodal tasks, will reveal systematic failure modes in model faithfulness and bias that are not captured by current task-specific or automated metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark dataset by augmenting existing multimodal summarization corpora (e.g., How2, TVSum) with fine-grained human annotations for faithfulness (factual alignment between source modalities and summary) and social biases (e.g., stereotyping in generated descriptions of people). Building on the STORYSUMM annotation protocol, we will design a novel annotation scheme for cross-modal consistency. We will then evaluate a suite of state-of-the-art multimodal models (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2) on this benchmark, analyzing correlations between automated metrics (e.g., CLIPScore, BLEURT) and human judgments to propose a standardized evaluation recipe.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) the first publicly available, human-annotated benchmark for holistic evaluation of multimodal summarization, 2) a detailed analysis of faithfulness and bias failures in current models, and 3) a set of validated, practical guidelines and metric recommendations for standardized multimodal summarization evaluation, directly extending the researcher's prior work on STORYSUMM and PERSONA into the multimodal domain.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal evaluation often focuses on task performance (e.g., VQA accuracy), this proposal uniquely adapts the nuanced, human-centered evaluation frameworks from text summarization—specifically faithfulness and pluralistic alignment—to the multimodal setting, creating a unified lens to assess both factual integrity and social impact.

proposals.title: HELM-XL: Scaling Holistic Evaluation to Trillion-Parameter Language Models
proposals.motivation: As language models scale to trillions of parameters, existing evaluation frameworks like the original HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) face critical scalability bottlenecks. Pre-2026, evaluations often rely on limited, static benchmarks that fail to capture emergent capabilities, novel failure modes, and the disproportionate resource demands of testing such massive models. A systematic, efficient, and scalable evaluation methodology is urgently needed to guide the development and responsible deployment of these frontier-scale systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a stratified, task-adaptive sampling strategy and a distributed, parallel evaluation infrastructure, we can create a scalable version of HELM that provides a faithful, multi-dimensional performance profile for trillion-parameter models while remaining computationally tractable.
proposals.method: We will extend the HELM framework by designing a two-tiered evaluation strategy. First, we will develop a dynamic task sampler that uses model performance on a small, diverse probe set to select the most informative scenarios from the full HELM taxonomy for deeper evaluation, minimizing redundant computation. Second, we will engineer a distributed evaluation harness leveraging model parallelism (e.g., Tensor Parallelism, Pipeline Parallelism) to efficiently run inference and scoring across hundreds of scenarios. We will integrate specific tests for known scaling phenomena, such as instruction-following granularity and reasoning chain faithfulness, building on our prior work in fine-grained supervision and faithfulness evaluation (STORYSUMM).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) HELM-XL, the first publicly available, scalable evaluation framework specifically designed for trillion-parameter models; 2) a new dataset of performance profiles and failure analyses for the largest publicly disclosed models as of 2025; and 3) methodological insights into cost-effective, holistic evaluation at the extreme scale of AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While HELM established holistic evaluation, scaling it efficiently to trillion-parameter models is an unsolved systems and methodology challenge. Our approach is novel in its adaptive sampling to reduce the evaluation cost function and its tight integration with the parallel computing architectures required to run these models, moving beyond simply applying existing benchmarks on larger hardware.

proposals.title: Succession-Based Training: An Ecological Analogy for Emergent Summarization from Unstructured Text
