proposals.motivation: Current summarization models are predominantly trained on datasets with clear source-summary pairs, often derived from structured or semi-structured text (e.g., news articles). This creates a bias towards generating summaries that follow rigid templates, potentially harming faithfulness and adaptability when summarizing highly unstructured, narrative, or conversational text. A new paradigm is needed to improve model robustness and faithfulness for diverse, real-world text formats where salient information is fluidly distributed.
proposals.hypothesis: Guiding a model's learning through a curriculum that mimics ecological succession—starting from exposure to raw, unstructured text and gradually introducing soft, content-based constraints—will lead to more faithful, flexible, and context-sensitive summarization than training with explicit, structured instructions.
proposals.method: We will design a novel training curriculum for a transformer-based model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only LLM). First, a pre-training phase will involve masked language modeling on a large corpus of unstructured narratives (e.g., STORYSUMM sources). Then, a fine-tuning phase will implement 'succession signals': instead of direct summary targets, the model will receive iterative, soft constraints (e.g., via auxiliary losses) to first identify key entities, then their relations, and finally salient event chains, allowing a coherent summary to emerge from these learned representations. We will adapt contrastive learning and fine-grained supervision methods from the researcher's prior work to implement these gradual guidance signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, ecologically-inspired training paradigm for NLP, 2) a new dataset or benchmark for evaluating faithfulness in summarization of unstructured narratives, extending the STORYSUMM framework, and 3) empirical evidence on how training structure affects a model's ability to capture implicit narrative flow and relational dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual shift from enforcing a fixed summary structure to cultivating conditions for emergent summarization, drawing a direct analogy from restoration ecology. Technically, it moves beyond standard instruction fine-tuning or prompt engineering by designing a multi-stage, constraint-based curriculum where the final task structure is not pre-defined.

proposals.title: Homology-Guided Summarization Transfer: A Bio-Inspired Framework for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Summarization
proposals.motivation: Zero-shot cross-lingual summarization for unseen language pairs remains a significant challenge, especially for low-resource and typologically distant languages. Current methods often rely on multilingual LLMs without explicit linguistic guidance, leading to unpredictable and often poor performance. This research addresses the need for a principled, linguistically-grounded transfer method to improve summarization quality for languages with no direct supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling linguistic homology—the structural and functional similarity between languages—we can create a predictive framework to guide the zero-shot transfer of summarization capabilities from high-resource to low-resource languages more effectively than existing distance-agnostic approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first quantifies linguistic homology using a multi-faceted distance metric combining syntactic features from Universal Dependencies, typological features from databases like WALS, and embedding-space distances from models like LASER3. This homology score will then be used to condition a parameter-efficient adapter module (e.g., a HyperNetwork or a Mixture-of-Experts layer) within a multilingual encoder-decoder model (e.g., mT5 or mBART). The adapter will project target-language representations onto a learned 'functional manifold' optimized for summarization in source languages, enabling controlled transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, interpretable metric for predicting zero-shot summarization transferability between languages, and 2) a new model architecture that uses this metric to guide adaptation, leading to more robust performance on unseen, distant languages. We will also release a new evaluation benchmark covering diverse language families to test the framework.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and methodological analogy to protein homology modeling, translating a bioinformatics solution to an NLP problem. Unlike prior work that uses linguistic distance as a post-hoc explanation, our method actively uses a composite homology measure as a predictive signal to steer model adaptation.

proposals.title: Anchoring Alignment: Establishing Semantic Consensus for Language Models via a Core Concept Lexicon
proposals.motivation: Current methods for aligning language models (LMs) or vision-language models (VLMs) often rely on image grounding, preference tuning, or human feedback, which can be resource-intensive and introduce specific biases. This project addresses a core, underexplored question: can we achieve robust semantic alignment between models purely through shared textual understanding of fundamental concepts, without relying on a shared sensory modality like vision? This is critical for developing more efficient, interpretable, and less biased alignment techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by training disparate models to produce aligned embeddings for a curated set of core semantic concepts, we can induce a shared, high-dimensional semantic space, enabling alignment that generalizes to related concepts and tasks without direct cross-modal supervision.
proposals.method: We will first construct a dataset, the Core Semantic Lexicon (CSL), comprising diverse, high-quality textual descriptions for a foundational set of concepts (e.g., from WordNet or psycholinguistic norms). We will then fine-tune separate LMs (e.g., LLaMA-2, Mistral) or a VLM's text encoder using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) to maximize agreement between their embeddings for descriptions of the same concept while distancing embeddings for different concepts. Alignment success will be evaluated via downstream tasks like semantic similarity judgment and zero-shot cross-model inference, benchmarking against image-anchored baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for text-only semantic alignment inspired by linguistic comparative methods, 2) the CSL dataset for benchmarking semantic alignment, and 3) empirical evidence on the feasibility and limits of modality-agnostic alignment, advancing the field beyond reliance on visual anchors.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of the linguistic comparative method to model alignment and its explicit challenge to the assumed necessity of visual grounding for establishing semantic consensus, proposing a purely textual pathway.

proposals.title: Aligning Values Through Text: Data-Efficient, Multi-Perspective Narrative Tuning for Abstract Concept Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Current alignment methods for large language models (LLMs) often rely on massive-scale instruction tuning or multimodal grounding, which is resource-intensive and may conflate sensory correlation with abstract ethical reasoning. This work challenges two dominant assumptions: that effective alignment requires vast data and that grounding in visual or embodied experience is necessary for learning concepts like harm and intent. A successful demonstration would offer a more efficient, interpretable, and scalable path to value alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model can acquire robust, human-aligned abstract concepts (e.g., fairness, harm, intent) through fine-tuning on a small, semantically dense corpus of multi-perspective textual narratives, achieving alignment performance comparable to models trained on orders-of-magnitude more data or multimodal inputs.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel dataset, the 'Narrative Alignment Corpus' (NAC), comprising thousands of rich textual scenarios. Each scenario will include detailed descriptions of actions, diverse character perspectives, ethical reasoning, and consequences, drawing from pre-2026 frameworks like STORYSUMM for faithfulness and PERSONA for pluralism. We will fine-tune a base pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2 or T5) exclusively on NAC using standard supervised fine-tuning and contrastive learning objectives to reinforce desired reasoning patterns. Evaluation will leverage existing benchmarks (e.g., ETHICS, TruthfulQA) and a new human evaluation protocol inspired by the researcher's prior work on fine-grained supervision and faithfulness.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, publicly released dataset for data-efficient alignment research; 2) empirical evidence on the sufficiency of text-only, high-quality data for abstract concept alignment; and 3) a new methodology for evaluating the transfer and robustness of aligned reasoning in LLMs, advancing beyond simple preference scoring.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely combines the ideas of extreme data efficiency and purely semantic alignment, divorcing abstract value learning from both massive scale and sensory grounding, which is a significant departure from contemporary scaling laws and embodied AI hypotheses.

proposals.title: Diagnosing and Disentangling Cross-Lingual Alignment Noise for Faithful Summarization
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual summarization and alignment tasks rely on parallel corpora, which are known to contain diverse imperfections beyond simple random noise. Pre-2026 work on summarization evaluation (e.g., STORYSUMM) and dataset biases highlights that treating all misalignment as uniform noise leads to models that are robust but semantically blunt, failing to preserve nuanced meaning. A fine-grained diagnosis of alignment errors is crucial for building systems that are both robust and faithful, especially for low-resource languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and classifying distinct types of cross-lingual alignment imperfections (e.g., annotation errors, word-order divergence, idiomatic mismatch) will enable more semantically aware and faithful model adaptations than treating all noise as a single category.
proposals.method: We will first create a diagnostic dataset by annotating imperfections in existing cross-lingual summarization/translation pairs (e.g., from XL-Sum) with error types. Using this, we will train a multi-class classifier (based on a pre-trained multilingual encoder like XLM-R) to tag sentence pairs. For each error type, we will design specialized sub-modules: robust attention masking via dropout for random noise, syntactic reordering networks for word-order issues, and constrained paraphrase generators (fine-tuned mT5) for idiomatic mismatches. These modules will be integrated into a controllable adapter layer for a sequence-to-sequence model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel typology and annotated benchmark for cross-lingual alignment imperfections, 2) a modular, diagnosable model architecture that applies targeted corrections based on error type, and 3) new insights into the relationship between specific data imperfections and model faithfulness in cross-lingual generation tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on data cleaning or robust training that treats noise monolithically, this approach disentangles the *semantic nature* of misalignment, enabling explainable, type-specific interventions rather than blanket robustness.

proposals.title: Robust Cross-Lingual Alignment via Iterative Correspondence Refinement and Loss Truncation
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual NLP models rely heavily on high-quality parallel data, which is scarce for most language pairs. Automatically mined or weakly aligned bitext is noisy, containing misaligned sentences and outliers that degrade model performance. This work addresses the critical need for training algorithms robust to such imperfect supervision, enabling effective cross-lingual learning for a broader set of languages and domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling cross-lingual learning as a joint optimization of transformation and correspondence—inspired by point set registration—will yield more robust alignment from noisy parallel data than standard methods assuming fixed, clean pairs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Robust Iterative Closest Point (ICP) framework to NLP. Source and target sentence embeddings form two point sets. The model will iteratively: 1) compute a soft probabilistic correspondence matrix using a cross-lingual similarity measure, 2) estimate a linear (or shallow non-linear) transformation to align the spaces using weighted least squares, and 3) apply a robust loss function (e.g., a truncated loss) to down-weight outlier pairs identified by low correspondence probability. This loop will be integrated into the training of a contrastive or mapping-based cross-lingual model.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a novel training algorithm for robust cross-lingual representation learning, a systematic evaluation of its performance on noisy datasets (e.g., mined bitext), and insights into the biases and failure modes of standard alignment methods when correspondences are imperfect.
proposals.novelty: While cross-lingual alignment is well-studied, explicitly and iteratively co-learning the instance-level correspondence matrix with the transformation during training is a novel approach, directly importing and adapting robust estimation techniques from computer vision to this NLP problem.

proposals.title: CLEANER: Closed-Loop Enhancement of Alignment via Noisy Iterative Refinement
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual alignment is crucial for tasks like multilingual summarization and dataset creation, but high-quality parallel data is scarce for most language pairs. Unsupervised methods generate noisy pseudo-parallel corpora, which degrade downstream model performance. Current approaches either treat noise as a nuisance to be filtered or ignore it, lacking a systematic way to learn from and correct alignment imperfections inherent in real-world, low-resource scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative, closed-loop system that trains alignment-tolerant models on deliberately noisy pseudo-parallel data can both improve the robustness of cross-lingual representations and actively refine the quality of the synthetic alignment data itself, leading to superior performance in low-resource settings.
proposals.method: We will build an iterative framework starting with an unsupervised MT system (e.g., mBART or a denoising autoencoder) to create an initial noisy pseudo-corpus. We will deliberately model realistic noise patterns (sentence shuffling, partial alignments). This data will train a robust alignment model, potentially using noise-invariant objectives like contrastive learning with hard negative mining or a noise-aware attention mechanism. The improved model will then be used to score and filter alignment candidates, and to generate cleaner synthetic translations for the next iteration, creating a self-improving cycle.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel iterative framework for unsupervised cross-lingual alignment that actively learns to correct noise. 2) New methods for training alignment models robust to structured noise, advancing the field of low-resource NLP. 3) A reproducible evaluation protocol, extending principles from PERSONA and STORYSUMM, to measure faithfulness and robustness of alignments in tasks like cross-lingual summarization.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats data cleaning and model training as separate stages, this proposal integrates them into a synergistic loop where each component improves the other. It moves beyond merely tolerating noise to using the model's learned representations to explicitly disentangle and correct alignment errors.

proposals.title: PhyloGap: A Phylogenetic Gap Analysis and Hybrid Collection Framework for Low-Resource Summarization Data
proposals.motivation: Current summarization datasets like WikiLingua exhibit severe linguistic bias, heavily favoring high-resource languages and leaving many language families underrepresented. This gap hinders the development and evaluation of summarization models for low-resource languages, perpetuating inequity in NLP. A systematic, principled approach to dataset expansion is needed to ensure diverse and representative coverage.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting phylogenetic gap analysis from conservation biology to map linguistic resource disparities, and deploying a hybrid expert-crowdsourced collection framework, we can create a more phylogenetically diverse and higher-quality summarization dataset for low-resource languages than unstructured expansion efforts.
proposals.method: First, we will construct a language phylogeny using Glottolog and perform a gap analysis on WikiLingua, quantifying coverage per family and prioritizing underrepresented branches. For high-priority languages, we will design a two-tier collection: 1) Expert-led annotation of a high-quality core set, using guidelines from STORYSUMM for faithfulness. 2) A scalable crowdsourcing platform, gamified to engage native speakers, that bootstraps data from existing parallel texts (e.g., religious scriptures, UN documents) and uses quality control methods inspired by pluralistic alignment testbeds like PERSONA.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel methodology for systematic dataset expansion in NLP and a significantly expanded, phylogenetically diverse version of WikiLingua. It will also provide new insights into the challenges of cross-lingual summarization data collection and evaluation, particularly regarding faithfulness in low-resource contexts.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the formal adaptation of phylogenetic gap analysis—a biodiversity informatics tool—to diagnose and address linguistic data scarcity, combined with a hybrid collection model that strategically integrates expert curation with community-driven crowdsourcing.

proposals.title: TopoAlign: Cross-Lingual Article-Summary Alignment via Contrastive Semantic Topology Mapping
proposals.motivation: Creating high-quality multilingual summarization datasets is bottlenecked by the need for expert human annotation. Current automatic methods for aligning articles with summaries across languages rely heavily on lexical overlap or sentence embeddings, which fail to capture deep semantic and rhetorical equivalence, especially for long-form text. This limits the scale and quality of training data for summarization models in low-resource languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent semantic and rhetorical structure of a text can be modeled as a topology, and that a contrastive learning framework can learn to align the conserved 'semantic core' between an article and its summary across languages, analogous to how TM-align matches protein folds despite sequence divergence.
proposals.method: We will adapt the TM-align algorithm's core principle of maximizing topological overlap scores. First, we will encode document-summary pairs (in multiple languages) using pre-trained multilingual language models (e.g., mT5, XLM-R) to generate contextualized token representations. We will then construct a graph topology for each text, where nodes are semantic units (e.g., sentences or clauses) and edges represent rhetorical or semantic relations (e.g., using discourse parsers or attention patterns). A novel neural scorer, trained via contrastive learning with hard negatives, will learn to compute a Topological Similarity Score (TSS) by identifying and aligning conserved sub-graph motifs, optimizing for the alignment of the semantic core rather than surface features.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for automatically constructing and verifying multilingual article-summary pairs based on deep semantic topology, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual summary faithfulness that moves beyond n-gram or embedding similarity, and 3) a publicly released dataset of aligned multilingual article-summary pairs for under-resourced languages.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and methodological analogy from protein structure alignment to semantic text alignment, and in explicitly modeling and aligning the topological structure of discourse for cross-lingual tasks, which is underexplored compared to sentence- or token-level alignment.

proposals.title: Aligning Summaries Across Languages: A Fine-Grained, Content-Based Approach for Multilingual Dataset Creation
proposals.motivation: High-quality multilingual summarization datasets are scarce, often created via image-matching heuristics or direct translation, which can introduce content mismatches or cultural biases. This limits the development and faithful evaluation of summarization models for low-resource languages. A robust, content-driven alignment method is needed to create accurate article-summary pairs across languages, directly addressing the evaluation challenges highlighted in works like STORYSUMM.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-grained, content-based alignment using multilingual sentence embeddings and semantic similarity, guided by structured content units, can more accurately identify and pair cross-lingual article-summary equivalents than existing heuristic or translation-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first decomposes source articles and candidate summaries (from a multilingual corpus like WikiLingua) into structured content units (e.g., main events, entities, claims). Using pre-trained multilingual encoders (e.g., LaBSE, Sentence-BERT), we will compute fine-grained semantic similarity between units across languages. We will then apply a graph-matching algorithm (e.g., optimal transport or maximum weight matching) to align units and compute an overall document-pair fidelity score, creating a high-confidence aligned dataset. This method will be compared against baseline image-matching and translation-based alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, reproducible methodology for creating high-fidelity multilingual summarization datasets, 2) a new benchmark dataset with verified cross-lingual alignments for multiple languages, and 3) an analysis of alignment biases introduced by different methods, extending the pluralistic alignment perspective from PERSONA to the cross-lingual setting.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond coarse image-matching heuristics by applying fine-grained, content-focused alignment techniques—inspired by Dr. Ladhak's prior work on fine-grained supervision—specifically to the cross-lingual summarization data creation problem, which is underexplored.

proposals.title: Semantic Pharmacophores for Cross-Lingual Summarization: A Latent Alignment Framework
proposals.motivation: Direct cross-lingual summarization (CLS) aims to generate a summary in a target language from a source-language document, bypassing full translation. Current pre-2026 approaches often rely on cascaded translation-and-summarize pipelines, which propagate errors and are inefficient, or on multilingual models that may not explicitly enforce semantic alignment. There is a need for methods that directly learn and compress the core semantics shared across languages to improve faithfulness and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: By learning to encode documents into a language-agnostic latent representation that captures only the abstract, summary-critical semantic features—analogous to a pharmacophore in drug discovery—a model can generate more faithful and fluent target-language summaries directly, outperforming cascade-based and naive multilingual baselines.
proposals.method: We will adapt the encoder-decoder Transformer architecture. The encoder will be trained with a novel bottleneck objective to produce a 'semantic pharmacophore' vector, enforced via contrastive learning against negative examples (e.g., semantically different documents) and alignment losses using translated sentence pairs as supervision. The decoder will be initialized from a target-language summarization model and trained to reconstruct target summaries from this latent representation. We will leverage existing CLS datasets (e.g., XL-Sum) and evaluate with automatic metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore) and the faithfulness-focused protocols from STORYSUMM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel modeling framework for CLS based on explicit semantic compression, 2) a new dataset analysis highlighting cross-lingual semantic alignment biases, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between direct latent alignment and cascade methods for summary quality and faithfulness.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and technical adaptation of the pharmacophore model—a method for abstracting functional features across diverse molecular structures—to cross-lingual NLP, creating an explicit, compressed semantic intermediary for summarization, which is distinct from standard multilingual embedding spaces or pipeline approaches.

proposals.title: Self-Augmenting Models at the Edge: A Closed-Loop Back-Translation System with Tiny ModernBERT
proposals.motivation: Deploying performant NLP models on edge devices is constrained by limited compute, data scarcity for specific domains or user dialects, and privacy concerns with cloud-based training. While efficient architectures like ModernBERT address size, and back-translation is a proven data augmentation technique, they remain separate paradigms. A self-contained system that can generate its own relevant training data and learn from it on-device would enable personalized, private, and adaptive language understanding without connectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: A tiny ModernBERT model can be effectively adapted into a self-contained, on-device back-translation engine, and the synthetic data it generates from local text can be used to fine-tune the same model, leading to measurable gains in task performance on local language patterns without external data or compromising the model's efficiency.
proposals.method: We will instantiate a tiny ModernBERT model (e.g., <10M parameters) and train it for a pivot-based back-translation task (e.g., EN->FR->EN) using a small, general-purpose parallel corpus. This model will be deployed in a simulated edge environment. Using a local seed of target-language text (e.g., user queries), the model will generate synthetic source sentences via the pivot. We will then perform on-device continual fine-tuning using a contrastive or masked language modeling objective on this synthetic, in-domain data, evaluating the model's performance on held-out local data before and after the self-augmentation cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel system design for closed-loop, self-supervised learning on edge devices, 2) a new evaluation framework for measuring on-device adaptation to local linguistic domains, and 3) empirical analysis of the trade-offs between synthetic data quality, model size, and adaptation gains in a privacy-preserving setting.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a single, ultra-efficient model as both the generator and consumer of its own synthetic training data in a closed, on-device loop is a novel systems-learning paradigm, moving beyond using back-translation as a static, offline data augmentation step.

proposals.title: Back-Translation for Faithful Summarization in Low-Resource Languages: A Data Augmentation and Evaluation Study
proposals.motivation: High-quality summarization datasets are scarce for low-resource languages, hindering the development and evaluation of faithful summarization models. This limitation is critical, as summarization is a key tool for information access. Existing methods like direct translation of English datasets can introduce cultural and linguistic biases, compromising faithfulness evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using back-translation through a pivot language (e.g., English) to generate synthetic source articles in a low-resource language, paired with their original summaries, will create more linguistically and culturally appropriate training and evaluation data. This will improve both the performance and the measurable faithfulness of summarization models for that language.
proposals.method: We will select a low-resource language (e.g., Bengali or Swahili) and an existing high-quality summarization dataset (e.g., XL-Sum). Using a strong pre-2026 multilingual model (e.g., mT5 or mBART), we will back-translate the English summaries to the target language to create synthetic source articles. We will rigorously evaluate the quality of this synthetic data and use it to fine-tune summarization models. Faithfulness will be evaluated using adapted versions of pre-2026 metrics (e.g., QAFactEval, entity-based metrics) and human assessment, following methodologies from STORYSUMM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel methodology for creating linguistically grounded summarization data for low-resource languages, 2) an analysis of how synthetic data impacts model faithfulness compared to translated-data baselines, and 3) a new testbed for pluralistic alignment in summarization, extending the PERSONA framework to cross-lingual faithfulness.
proposals.novelty: While back-translation is established in machine translation, its application to generate *source documents* for summarization is under-explored. The novelty lies in explicitly targeting the *faithfulness* dimension in a low-resource setting, bridging Ladhak's expertise in evaluation (STORYSUMM) and pluralistic alignment (PERSONA) with data augmentation.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Neural Machine Translation via Iterative Bootstrapping and Cycle-Consistent Refinement
proposals.motivation: High-quality parallel data is a critical bottleneck for machine translation (MT) for most language pairs, limiting the application of powerful neural models. While unsupervised MT methods using cross-lingual word embeddings and back-translation exist, their performance plateaus due to error propagation from noisy initial alignments and pseudo-data. A robust, iterative self-correction mechanism is needed to break this ceiling without any parallel supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling unsupervised MT as an iterative refinement process—inspired by ab initio protein structure prediction—where an initial noisy alignment is progressively corrected via cycle-consistency filtering and model retraining, will yield significant gains in translation fidelity and robustness over single-pass methods.
proposals.method: We adapt the iterative fragment assembly paradigm from Rosetta. First, we create an initial cross-lingual embedding alignment using adversarial training (e.g., MUSE) on monolingual corpora. This alignment seeds a phrase-table or initial NMT model to generate a first-pass pseudo-parallel corpus. The core innovation is a bootstrapping loop: we perform forward and back-translation, compute sentence-level cycle-consistency loss (e.g., BERTScore or reconstruction BLEU), and use this to filter low-confidence pairs. The filtered, higher-quality corpus then refines the embedding alignment (via iterative Procrustes) and retrains the translation model. This cycle repeats, progressively improving data and model quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel iterative bootstrapping framework for unsupervised MT that explicitly minimizes error accumulation, 2) a new, high-quality evaluation benchmark focused on translation faithfulness across iterative cycles, extending the STORYSUMM evaluation paradigm to MT, and 3) an analysis of how refinement cycles affect different types of translation biases.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal adaptation of an iterative refinement and conformational sampling strategy from computational biology to unsupervised MT, introducing a closed-loop, self-correcting training cycle driven by cycle-consistency filtering, which moves beyond static back-translation.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Calibration: Unsupervised MT for Multilingual Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Efficient deployment of multilingual models like ModernBERT on edge devices requires aggressive compression via quantization and pruning. These techniques rely on calibration data that is representative of the model's operational domain, but such data is scarce for many languages, especially low-resource ones. This creates a critical bottleneck for global, equitable deployment of efficient NLP models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised machine translation (UMT) can synthesize high-quality, pseudo-parallel text across languages, and that this synthetic multilingual corpus will serve as a superior calibration dataset for post-training compression, preserving cross-lingual performance better than monolingual or English-only calibration data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-2026 UMT framework (e.g., MASS or XLM) to generate a large-scale, multi-way pseudo-parallel corpus spanning high- and low-resource languages. This corpus will be used as the calibration dataset for standard post-training quantization (e.g., GPTQ) and magnitude pruning of a ModernBERT model. We will rigorously evaluate the compressed model's performance on cross-lingual understanding benchmarks (like XNLI and XQuAD) and summarization faithfulness (extending STORYSUMM metrics) across languages, comparing against baselines using English-only or crawled monolingual calibration data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, data-centric method for compressing multilingual models that mitigates the low-resource language calibration bottleneck; 2) a systematic evaluation framework for assessing compression-induced biases in cross-lingual performance; and 3) a publicly released, synthetic multi-way parallel corpus for calibration and related tasks.
proposals.novelty: While UMT is established for translation and calibration data is known to be crucial, the novel fusion lies in repurposing UMT explicitly as a data synthesis engine for model compression, creating a targeted solution for the multilingual efficiency problem that does not require parallel human data.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Pseudo-Parallel Data Creation for Faithful Summarization Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the faithfulness of abstractive summaries is a critical, unsolved challenge in NLP, as highlighted by datasets like STORYSUMM. High-quality, human-annotated data for training or fine-tuning faithfulness evaluation models is scarce and expensive to produce, especially across diverse domains and narrative styles. This data bottleneck limits the development and robustness of automated evaluation metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised machine translation techniques, specifically back-translation and iterative refinement with denoising autoencoders, can generate high-quality pseudo-parallel data (source text <-> faithful summary pairs) from monolingual corpora, and that models trained on this synthetic data will achieve competitive performance on human-annotated faithfulness benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the unsupervised neural machine translation framework of Lample et al. (2018) to the summarization domain. A shared encoder-decoder architecture will be trained on two monolingual corpora: one of source documents and one of human-written (but not necessarily parallel) summaries. Using back-translation, we will generate synthetic summaries for source documents and vice-versa, creating a pseudo-parallel corpus. This corpus will then be used to fine-tune a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model (e.g., BART or T5) to specifically generate or score faithful summaries, with evaluation against benchmarks like STORYSUMM and FRANK.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel methodology for generating synthetic faithfulness-focused training data without human annotation, 2) a new model for faithfulness evaluation or generation trained on this data, and 3) an analysis of the biases and failure modes introduced by this unsupervised data creation process, extending research on pluralistic alignment and evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While unsupervised MT is established, its application to create data for the intrinsically different task of summarization faithfulness evaluation is novel. This approach directly addresses the data scarcity problem in a domain where parallel data is defined by abstractive transformation and factual alignment, not linguistic translation.

proposals.title: Massively Multilingual Pre-training and Bias Propagation: A Scale Analysis in Summarization
proposals.motivation: Prior work, including the researcher's own on STORYSUMM and PERSONA, has established that language models propagate and amplify societal biases, often studied in monolingual or bilingual contexts. The rise of massively multilingual pre-trained models (e.g., mT5, BLOOM, XLM-R) trained on hundreds of languages introduces a new variable: does the scale and diversity of the training data fundamentally alter how biases are encoded and transferred across languages? Understanding this is critical for deploying these models globally in high-stakes applications like summarization, where biased content can have real-world consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that massively multilingual pre-training does not uniformly mitigate bias propagation; instead, it creates a complex, non-linear relationship where bias manifests and transfers asymmetrically across language clusters, influenced by data volume, linguistic proximity, and cultural representation in the pre-training corpus.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scale analysis using massively multilingual encoder-decoder models (mT5, mBART) of varying sizes. Building on the STORYSUMM framework, we will create or adapt multilingual summarization test suites with embedded bias probes (e.g., gender, geo-cultural stereotypes) across typologically diverse languages. We will fine-tune models on a neutral summarization task and evaluate bias propagation using faithfulness metrics and targeted stereotype scores, analyzing correlations with pre-training data statistics (e.g., per-language token count, source diversity).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of bias propagation as a function of multilingual pre-training scale, offering empirical evidence on whether 'more languages' equates to 'fairer models.' It will contribute a novel evaluation methodology and dataset for multilingual bias in generation, directly informing safer development and deployment practices for global NLP applications.
proposals.novelty: While bias in monolingual models is well-studied, this proposal uniquely shifts the focus to the interaction between extreme multilingualism and bias, moving beyond simple translation-based evaluations to study emergent, cross-lingual bias transfer patterns in generative tasks.

ideas.title: Benchmark more models and versions

ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: English-centric evaluation of language models
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that evaluation primarily in English is sufficient for global language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse problem: language generation
ideas.description: Use the interlingua as a controlled source for generating diverse paraphrases within a single language.
ideas.sourceTitle: A neural interlingua for multilingual machine translation

ideas.title: Unsupervised Iterative Refinement for Noisy Cross-Lingual Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction proposes an iterative, closed-loop framework that leverages unsupervised machine translation to generate initial pseudo-parallel corpora from non-parallel data, deliberately introducing or modeling realistic alignment noise (e.g., sentence-level misalignments, partial document alignments). The core innovation is to then train robust alignment-tolerant models on this noisy data, and use the improved representations and translation capabilities of these models to refine and clean the initial pseudo-parallel data in the next iteration. This creates a synergistic cycle where the unsupervised MT system becomes better at handling imperfection by learning from it, and the alignment-robust models are trained on progressively higher-quality, but still realistically noisy, synthetic data. The outcome is a system that does not merely tolerate noise but actively learns to disentangle and correct for it in low-resource cross-lingual scenarios.

ideas.title: Disentangle and Diagnose: Models for Typed Cross-Lingual Alignment Imperfections
ideas.description: This research develops cross-lingual models that do not treat alignment noise as a single problem, but instead learn to classify and handle distinct types of imperfections. The system identifies categories such as random annotation errors, word-order divergences, phrase-level idiomatic mismatches, and sentence-level conceptual gaps. For each diagnosed type, it applies a specialized sub-module—ranging from robust attention masking for noise to structured paraphrase generation for idioms. This results in models that are not merely robust, but semantically aware and capable of explaining their handling of misaligned data.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Machine Translation for Cross-Lingual Model Compression
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using unsupervised machine translation techniques not for translation itself, but as a data synthesis engine for model compression. It generates high-quality, pseudo-parallel text across multiple languages to serve as a diverse and representative calibration dataset for post-training quantization and pruning of models like ModernBERT. This addresses the critical bottleneck of obtaining suitable calibration data for compressing models intended for multilingual use, especially for low-resource languages. The result is a robust, general-purpose compression method that significantly reduces the model's memory footprint while maintaining its cross-lingual performance, enabling efficient deployment on edge devices worldwide.

ideas.title: Standardize evaluation for multimodal models
ideas.description: Extend the holistic evaluation framework to models processing both text and other modalities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Robust Compression for Cross-Lingual Models via Noise-Aware Post-Training
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how to aggressively compress a pre-trained multilingual language model (like ModernBERT) after training by explicitly designing the compression process to be robust to the noisy alignments inherent in its cross-lingual data. Instead of treating misalignments as a problem to be corrected before compression, the method will use them as a form of natural noise injection during the pruning and quantization phases, training the compression algorithm to preserve core semantic functions that are stable across alignment errors. This results in a model that is not only significantly smaller and faster but also more reliable when deployed on real-world, imperfectly aligned multilingual tasks. The core novelty is that the compression technique itself becomes a mechanism for enhancing cross-lingual robustness, a synergy neither standard compression nor standard alignment correction achieves alone.

ideas.title: Apply trade-off curve to other text generation tasks

ideas.sourceTitle: Faithful or Extractive? On Mitigating the Faithfulness-Abstractiveness Trade-off in Abstractive Summarization

ideas.title: Leveraging Protein Homology Mapping for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Summarization
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of investigating zero-shot cross-lingual summarization for unseen language pairs by drawing an analogy from computational biology. In bioinformatics, homology modeling solves the problem of predicting the structure/function of an unknown protein by mapping it to a known homologous structure. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing languages as sequences and summarization skill as a conserved functional trait. The adapted research idea is to develop a framework that explicitly measures linguistic homology (using syntactic, typological, and embedding-space metrics) and uses this to guide the projection or alignment of a target language's representations onto the 'functional manifold' of a source language summarization model, thereby creating a principled method for zero-shot transfer to linguistically distant, unseen languages.

ideas.title: Use style transfer metrics for paraphrase analysis
ideas.description: Adapt metrics from text style transfer to quantify the amount and quality of paraphrasing in summaries.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Large Language Models for News Summarization

ideas.title: The Retrieval-Infused Decoder: Specializing Generative Models for Post-Retrieval Reasoning
ideas.description: This research proposes a hybrid architecture where a standard decoder-only language model is augmented with a frozen, off-the-shelf bi-encoder retriever. The system is trained end-to-end, with the retriever providing a fixed set of candidate passages or token embeddings for each query. The decoder's attention mechanism is modified to explicitly attend to these retrieved representations as a contiguous, structured context block. The training objective forces the decoder to learn to identify and utilize relevant information from this retrieved set, even when it is noisy or incomplete, thereby specializing the model in the crucial task of evidence synthesis and verification that follows an efficient, but imperfect, retrieval step.

ideas.title: Cross-lingual Summarization via Shared Latent Semantic Compression
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational chemistry—specifically, pharmacophore modeling for scaffold hopping in drug discovery—to the problem of direct cross-lingual summarization. In the analogous field, the key innovation was abstracting diverse molecular structures into a shared latent representation of essential functional features to enable efficient cross-modal matching. The adapted idea is to develop a model that learns to encode source-language documents into a language-agnostic 'semantic pharmacophore,' a compressed latent representation capturing only the abstract features critical for summarization (e.g., core propositions, entity relationships, discourse roles). A decoder would then be trained to generate a coherent summary directly in the target language from this shared representation. This approach aims to improve efficiency and quality by eliminating the error-prone translation step and forcing the model to focus on aligning and compressing high-level semantics across languages.

ideas.title: Extend selector to more datasets and models
ideas.description: Apply the learned selector framework to a wider variety of datasets and summarization model architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Faithful or Extractive? On Mitigating the Faithfulness-Abstractiveness Trade-off in Abstractive Summarization

ideas.title: Apply back-translation for data augmentation

ideas.title: Data-Efficient Learning via Curriculum Synthesis and Selective Exposure
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that massive, undifferentiated pretraining data (e.g., 2 trillion tokens) is necessary for achieving state-of-the-art language model performance. Drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology and educational science, where structured curricula are proven to be more efficient for human learning than massive unstructured exposure, we propose adapting the concept of a 'synthesized curriculum' for model training. The analogous solution involves designing learning pathways that introduce concepts in a logical, scaffolded order with targeted practice. Adapted to AI, this means algorithmically generating or meticulously selecting a sequence of training tokens that teaches linguistic and reasoning constructs optimally, with strategic repetition and progressive complexity. The research would investigate whether models trained on a significantly smaller, but intelligently structured dataset (e.g., 100-500 billion tokens) can achieve Pareto improvements—matching or surpassing the capabilities of models trained on vastly larger, random corpora—thereby challenging the need for sheer data scale.

ideas.title: Semantic-Only Alignment via Data-Efficient, Multi-Modal Abstraction
ideas.description: This research investigates whether a model can learn human-aligned values and reasoning (alignment) using only text, but from a radically small, high-quality dataset. The core hypothesis is that by training on a carefully constructed corpus of rich, multi-perspective textual narratives describing actions, consequences, and ethical reasoning—without any paired images—the model can develop abstract, transferable concepts of harm, preference, and intent. This approach simultaneously challenges the necessity of visual grounding for alignment and the requirement for trillion-token-scale pretraining, proposing that alignment is more efficiently learned through semantic density and conceptual variation rather than through scale or sensory correlation.

ideas.title: HELM for extremely large models

ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Static benchmark for dynamic models
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that a static benchmark suffices for models that learn from user interactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Cross-Lingual Word Embedding Alignment via Iterative Bootstrapping and Cycle Consistency
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the field of Computational Biology, specifically ab initio protein structure prediction via iterative fragment assembly and refinement (e.g., Rosetta). The analogous problem is predicting a protein's 3D structure without a homologous template (supervision). The solution involves assembling short, plausible fragments from unrelated proteins into a decoy, then iteratively refining it through cycles of energy minimization. Adapted to unsupervised MT, the method would first create an initial, noisy alignment of cross-lingual semantic spaces (e.g., using adversarial training or statistical matching on monolingual corpora). This alignment is used to generate a first-pass pseudo-parallel corpus by translating word or phrase 'fragments'. A key innovation is the introduction of an iterative refinement cycle, inspired by conformational sampling in protein folding: sentences are translated forward and then back-translated, with the cycle-consistency loss (reconstruction error) used to filter out poor translations, improve the embedding alignment, and retrain the translation model in a bootstrapping loop. This creates a self-supervised feedback mechanism that progressively improves the quality of the pseudo-parallel data and the final translation model without any seed parallel sentences.

ideas.title: Semantic Alignment via Cross-Modal Lexical Anchoring
ideas.description: This idea challenges the necessity of image-based alignment by adapting a solution from historical linguistics. In that field, the comparative method aligns languages by identifying cognates through semantic and systematic sound correspondences, without needing a physical 'proto-language' artifact. Adapted here, we propose 'Semantic Alignment via Cross-Modal Lexical Anchoring.' The research would involve selecting a foundational set of core semantic concepts and training models (e.g., VLMs or disparate LLMs) to produce aligned text embeddings for diverse textual descriptions of these concepts. Success is measured by the models' agreement on the semantic relationships and properties of these anchor concepts, creating alignment purely through shared textual semantics, analogous to how languages are related through shared meaning, not shared sounds or images.

ideas.title: Apply a Biodiversity Survey Framework to Multilingual Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology, where biodiversity surveys assess ecosystem health across varied habitats, to the evaluation of multilingual language models. The analogous solution involves systematic sampling across environmental gradients to measure richness, evenness, and functional diversity. Adapted to NLP, this means constructing a stratified evaluation 'ecosystem' for ModernBERT that includes benchmarks across a wide range of language families (e.g., Indo-European, Afro-Asiatic, Sino-Tibetan, Niger-Congo), typological features (e.g., morphology, word order), resource availability, and task types (text classification, semantic retrieval). The outcome is a detailed 'performance biodiversity' map, revealing whether ModernBERT is a true multilingual generalist or has systematic weaknesses correlated with specific linguistic or resource dimensions, guiding more targeted future model development.

ideas.title: Cross-lingual Semantic Alignment via Contrastive Topological Mapping
ideas.description: This research seed proposes investigating cross-lingual alignment methods for creating multilingual article-summary pairs by drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically protein structure alignment. In that field, tools like TM-align successfully compare 3D protein structures with divergent sequences by focusing on aligning conserved topological cores and motifs, using a scoring function that measures overall fold similarity. Adapting this solution, the research would develop techniques that treat the latent semantic and rhetorical structure of a text as a topology. The goal is to create a model that learns to identify and align the conserved 'semantic core' and 'discourse motifs' between an article and its potential summary across different languages. This would be achieved through a contrastive learning framework designed to maximize the topological similarity of their meaning representations, moving beyond surface-level matching (like words or shared images) to enable robust alignment based on deep semantic equivalence.

ideas.title: On-Device Back-Translation for Tiny ModernBERT: Self-Supervised Data Augmentation at the Edge
ideas.description: This research fuses the architectural efficiency of a tiny ModernBERT with the data generation strategy of back-translation to create a self-contained, on-device learning system. The core idea is to deploy the tiny ModernBERT model on an edge device, where it can perform back-translation locally: translating a small seed of target-language text (e.g., user queries or local documents) into a pivot language and back again to generate synthetic, in-domain source text. This synthetic data is then used to continually fine-tune the same compact model via on-device learning, adapting it to local language patterns and domains without compromising privacy or requiring cloud connectivity. The synergy creates a closed-loop system where a single efficient model both generates its own training data and learns from it, enabling personalized and context-aware language understanding in low-resource or privacy-sensitive edge computing scenarios.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Phylogenetic Expansion for Low-Resource Language Dataset Creation
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from conservation biology—specifically the phylogenetic gap analysis and hybrid collection model used by biodiversity informatics platforms like GBIF—to the problem of expanding WikiLingua to include more low-resource languages. The analogous field addresses documenting under-sampled species in remote areas by systematically identifying knowledge gaps and then using a mix of expert-led efforts and scalable crowdsourcing. Adapted to this context, the idea involves first conducting a systematic 'language resource gap analysis' to map and prioritize underrepresented language families and regions. Subsequently, a hybrid framework for dataset creation is deployed: targeted, high-quality annotation projects led by computational linguists for a core set of critical languages, combined with a scalable, gamified crowdsourcing platform designed to engage native speaker communities globally. This platform would also strategically utilize existing but under-exploited resources, such as digitized historical documents and widely translated parallel texts (e.g., religious scriptures), to bootstrap data collection for the lowest-resource languages, ensuring a phylogenetically and geographically diverse expansion of the dataset.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Principles to Model Training for Unstructured Text
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that structured, step-by-step guide formats are superior for training or prompting models on tasks like text summarization, particularly for unstructured source material. Drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the field of restoration ecology and the principle of 'ecological succession,' we note that successful ecosystem recovery does not follow a rigid, pre-defined planting schedule but mimics the fluid, adaptive, and self-organizing process of natural succession. The analogous solution is to guide ecosystem development by creating conditions conducive to natural processes rather than enforcing a fixed structure. Adapted to the NLP research context, this suggests developing a novel training or prompting paradigm for summarization models. Instead of using structured 'how-to' steps, the model would be exposed first to completely unstructured text. Subsequent training signals or prompts would act like environmental conditions in succession, gently guiding the model to identify and connect salient concepts, allowing a summary structure to emerge organically based on the text's own content and relational dynamics, potentially leading to more flexible and context-sensitive summarization of less structured inputs.

ideas.title: Robust Cross-Lingual Learning via Point Set Registration Techniques
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of perfectly aligned parallel data for cross-lingual model training by drawing an analogy from computer vision, specifically point set registration (e.g., in medical image analysis). There, algorithms like Robust ICP solve the problem of aligning two 3D point sets with initially unknown and noisy correspondences by iteratively estimating pairings, computing a transformation, and rejecting outlier matches. Adapted to cross-lingual NLP, we propose developing models that treat source and target language embedding spaces as point sets. The training process would iteratively and jointly refine both the cross-lingual transformation (the alignment) and a probabilistic correspondence matrix between training instances, incorporating robustness mechanisms like loss truncation for potential misalignments. This creates a system robust to noisy or imperfectly aligned bitext, effectively learning from weakly supervised or automatically aligned data.

ideas.title: Sensitivity-Aware Non-Uniform Compression for ModernBERT
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond uniform post-training compression by first profiling the trained ModernBERT model to identify components critical for its core linguistic and task-specific knowledge. A small calibration set is used to compute sensitivity scores for different model parameters, generating a compression map. Pruning and quantization strategies are then applied selectively according to this map, preserving high precision in sensitive regions while aggressively compressing robust, redundant parameters. This results in a highly compressed model that maintains significantly higher accuracy on complex and diverse benchmarks compared to uniform compression baselines.

Models are trained to produce and interpret textual descriptions of these shared abstract anchors, forcing their semantic spaces to align relative to a common, non-linguistic sensory reference point. This ensures the alignment is grounded and prevents degenerate linguistic convergence, while maintaining the core efficiency of operating primarily through text semantics after the initial anchoring phase.

ideas.title: Conditional Computation for In-Context Retrieval in Decoder-Only Models
ideas.description: This research investigates a decoder-only model architecture augmented with specialized Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers to perform highly efficient, in-context retrieval. The model is trained to route different types of retrieval operations—such as term matching, semantic scoring, and query expansion—to distinct, sparse expert networks activated conditionally on the input. This allows a single, unified model to achieve the specialized, compute-efficient performance profiles typically associated with dedicated encoder-based retrievers, while retaining the decoder's ability to generate synthesized answers or follow-up queries. The core novelty is using conditional computation not just for capacity, but to create an internal, dynamic 'retrieval system' that can be optimized end-to-end for complex information-seeking tasks.

ideas.title: Linguistic-Capability-Aware TinyBERT Co-Designed with Hardware-Aware NAS and Knowledge Scaffolding
ideas.description: This research creates a high-performance tiny language model for edge devices by fundamentally rethinking the compression process. Instead of simply shrinking a large model, it uses hardware-aware Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to find optimal tiny sub-architectures specifically tailored to preserve distinct linguistic capabilities like syntax and semantics, guided by a suite of medium-sized teacher models. These specialized sub-models are integrated into a single, runtime-efficient architecture using a fused multi-expert system with switchable components. The final model is trained via progressive knowledge scaffolding, aligning its internal representations with those of the teachers, ensuring the tiny model retains robust performance on complex language tasks despite its severely constrained size.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Data-Efficiency Frontier: Isolating Architectural and Data-Curation Effects on Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This research rigorously tests the hypothesis that architectural innovations and sophisticated data curation can fundamentally alter scaling laws, enabling models trained on 200-500B tokens to match the multi-capability performance of those trained on 2T. We design a controlled ablation study across three axes: data quantity, data quality (via rigorous filtering and synthetic augmentation), and model architecture (targeting efficient long-context and reasoning). By training a family of models under a fixed compute budget and evaluating on a broad benchmark suite, we aim to precisely map the new data-efficiency frontier and identify the conditions under which massive pretraining becomes unnecessary for Pareto-optimal performance.

ideas.title: Hybrid Context-Aware PEFT: Diagnosing and Updating Long-Range Sensitive Components in ModernBERT
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple parameter count comparison to diagnose and address the specific architectural weaknesses of standard PEFT in long-context settings. We first profile ModernBERT to identify which components (e.g., attention mechanisms, positional encodings, normalization layers) exhibit the greatest sensitivity to increased sequence length during task adaptation. Based on this diagnosis, we design a hybrid PEFT system that strategically applies minimal, global updates to these long-range-critical components while using traditional local adapters for task-specific features. The final system is evaluated not just on overall performance, but on its ability to match full fine-tuning on metrics of long-range dependency modeling and positional reasoning across diverse long-context tasks.

ideas.title: Ensemble of Specialized Encoders via Committee-of-Experts Architecture
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a single, generalist encoder is necessary for optimal performance. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and the 'modularity of mind' hypothesis—which posits that the brain uses many specialized, domain-specific circuits rather than one general processor—we adapt this solution to machine learning. The analogous solution involves parallel, specialized modules whose outputs are integrated. The adapted idea is to study ensembles of smaller, specialized encoders, each optimized for a distinct data domain (e.g., scientific text, dialogue, code). A trainable gating network will learn to dynamically combine their contextualized representations based on input characteristics. This 'committee-of-experts' architecture aims to collectively outperform a single large model by leveraging targeted expertise and more efficient, modular computation, relaxing the requirement for a monolithic generalist encoder.

ideas.title: Decoder-Only Retrieval via Compressed Sensing Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that an explicit, optimized encoder is necessary for efficient retrieval by drawing an analogy from compressed sensing in signal processing. In that field, a sparse signal can be perfectly recovered from under-sampled measurements using reconstruction algorithms that leverage structural priors, bypassing traditional encoding requirements. Adapted to NLP, we propose treating the natural language query or prompt to a decoder-only model as a compressed 'measurement' of the target information. The model's forward pass then functions as the reconstruction algorithm, directly retrieving or generating the relevant content. This approach leverages the model's vast parametric knowledge as the structural prior, potentially allowing a single decoder to match the efficiency of a dedicated encoder-retriever system without architectural separation.

ideas.title: Adapting Hierarchical Memory Systems from Operating Systems for Long-Context ModernBERT
ideas.description: This research seed explores extending ModernBERT's context length beyond 8192 tokens by drawing an analogy from hierarchical memory systems in computer architecture and operating systems. The analogous problem—managing vast virtual address spaces with limited physical RAM—is solved through paging, caching, and hardware-managed translation (the MMU). We adapt this solution by proposing a 'Context Memory Hierarchy' for ModernBERT. The architecture would feature a primary, high-speed attention cache for a working set of tokens (e.g., 2048) and a secondary, larger memory bank holding the full long sequence (e.g., 32k+). A trainable 'attention management unit' (AMU) would act like an MMU, learning to predict and retrieve the most relevant context blocks from secondary memory into the primary cache for each layer or segment of computation. This approach aims to provide the model with efficient, selective access to a much longer context window, breaking the quadratic scaling barrier of standard attention by only applying full attention computations to a managed subset of the total context at any given time.

ideas.title: Input-Dependent Gated State-Space Models for Adaptive Long-Context Learning
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a hybrid architecture that overcomes the static nature of traditional SSMs by making their core dynamics input-dependent. The system uses a lightweight side network to generate the state transition and input matrices for the SSM based on a compressed view of the local context, creating a time-varying system that can adapt its behavior to different sequence patterns. A learned, data-controlled gating function is applied to the SSM output, allowing the model to selectively propagate or filter information. This design maintains the efficient O(N log N) or O(N) scaling for long sequences while achieving the expressive, context-sensitive processing typically reserved for attention.

ideas.title: Knowledge Distillation via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological 'nurse plant' strategy to the problem of distilling capabilities from large decoder-only models into the efficient ModernBERT architecture. In restoration ecology, hardy pioneer species are introduced first to prepare the environment for the desired climax species. Analogously, we propose training a small, specialized 'nurse' model on a curated subset of tasks (e.g., specific reasoning or structured knowledge tasks) where the large decoder's capabilities are most explicit. This intermediate model acts as a facilitator, transforming the decoder's complex, implicit knowledge into a more structured and accessible form. ModernBERT is then distilled from this intermediate 'nurse' model rather than directly from the massive decoder, creating a clearer learning pathway and potentially yielding a more capable and stable final model, thus improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the knowledge transfer process.

ideas.title: ModernBERT-Long: A Hybrid Router for Preserving Short-Context Performance While Extending to Long Sequences
ideas.description: This research proposes a hybrid architecture that extends ModernBERT's context window while preventing degradation of its core capabilities. A lightweight, learned router analyzes the input sequence to dynamically allocate processing between the original, frozen ModernBERT parameters and new, efficient long-context extension modules. This approach ensures that performance on established short-context benchmarks remains intact, while new parameters are specialized for long-sequence reasoning. The system is trained with a multi-objective loss that jointly optimizes for routing efficiency, long-context task performance, and the preservation of the original model's outputs on short inputs.

ideas.title: Robustness-Aware Neural Architecture Search for Generalizable Encoder Design
ideas.description: This approach automates the discovery of optimal encoder architectures by integrating explicit robustness and generalization metrics into the core Neural Architecture Search (NAS) process. The search algorithm performs multi-objective optimization, balancing standard accuracy against performance on out-of-distribution data and adversarial examples drawn from a fixed robustness benchmark. This ensures the discovered architectures are not merely overfitted to the search proxy but maintain stable feature representations under distribution shifts. The final system produces encoder blueprints that are inherently more reliable for deployment in unpredictable real-world environments.

ideas.title: Empirical scaling laws for ModernBERT via ecological population growth modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from population ecology to systematically analyze data scaling laws for the ModernBERT architecture. In ecology, scientists model how a population's size scales with a critical resource by collecting empirical data and fitting growth equations (like logistic or von Bertalanffy models) to reveal fundamental parameters such as intrinsic growth rates and carrying capacities. Analogously, we will train a series of ModernBERT models on carefully constructed, exponentially scaled subsets of a large corpus (e.g., from 1M to 1B tokens). We will then fit the resulting performance curves (plotting validation loss or downstream task accuracy against data size) with generalized scaling models—such as a power-law with a saturation term—to empirically derive the scaling exponents, identify performance saturation points, and map the efficient frontier for data investment, thereby providing a predictive framework for data scaling in modern encoders.

ideas.title: Targeted Adaptation via Sparse Biological Control for Long-Context Tuning
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that full fine-tuning is necessary for adapting ModernBERT to long-context tasks by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology. In metabolic engineering, a complex organism is repurposed not by rewriting its entire genome, but by making minimal, targeted genetic interventions at key regulatory points to redirect its existing machinery. Adapted to this context, the idea is to investigate if parameter-efficient fine-tuning can match full fine-tuning by moving beyond uniform methods like LoRA or prefix-tuning. Instead, we propose a biologically-inspired, targeted adaptation strategy: first, analyze ModernBERT's architecture to identify a sparse set of critical 'regulatory' parameters most influential for long-context processing (e.g., in attention mechanisms or specific network pathways). Then, develop a fine-tuning protocol that surgically updates only this identified subset. The core hypothesis is that this minimal, interpretable intervention—akin to inserting a small gene circuit—can efficiently redirect the model's powerful pre-existing capabilities to excel at long-context tasks, potentially matching the performance of full fine-tuning while being vastly more parameter-efficient.

ideas.title: Dynamic-Scale Encoder: A Single Model Architecture for Extreme Performance and Edge Deployment
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel, unified encoder-only architecture designed from the ground up for extreme scale elasticity. The core innovation is a training and distillation framework that first trains a massive-scale 'mother' model to explore the performance ceiling, then uses its internal representations and learned architectural principles to guide the co-training of a continuum of smaller, structurally-aligned sub-models. This creates a single model family where the largest variant achieves super-performance, the smallest variant runs efficiently on edge devices, and all intermediate sizes share a consistent architectural DNA, allowing for dynamic scaling based on compute constraints without catastrophic performance drops. The synergy enables a new paradigm of 'write once, deploy anywhere' for encoder models, where a single training run produces a full spectrum of deployable models with guaranteed behavioral consistency.

ideas.title: Architectural Scaling via Hierarchical Modularity for Encoder-Only Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the performance ceiling of a vastly larger encoder-only model by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In nature, organisms scale to massive sizes and complexity not by uniformly enlarging a single cell type, but by evolving hierarchical, modular structures like specialized organs and organ systems. Adapting this solution, we propose to scale encoder-only models not through dense parameter increases alone, but by architecting them as a hierarchy of specialized, interacting modules (e.g., for syntax, semantics, pragmatics, domain knowledge) with sparse, efficient interfaces. A meta-controller would dynamically route inputs and allocate computational resources among these modules. This approach, combined with ModernBERT's core optimizations, aims to break through the performance and efficiency ceilings of monolithic scaling, enabling super-performance on understanding tasks while maintaining trainability and inference feasibility.

ideas.title: Post-Training Model Compression via Astrophysical Data Reduction Techniques
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from astrophysical data reduction techniques to the post-training compression of ModernBERT. In astronomy, petabytes of raw telescope data are aggressively compressed after collection using lossy compression combined with intelligent, signal-preserving filters that prioritize scientifically valuable information over noise. Adapted to model compression, the research would first develop an analyzer to identify 'task-critical signals' within ModernBERT's parameters—mapping weights and activations to their importance for core NLP functionalities. Then, instead of uniform quantization/pruning, it would apply a multi-resolution, lossy compression scheme inspired by wavelet-based astronomical image formats. This scheme would compress different model components (e.g., layers, attention heads) at varying, adaptive ratios based on their functional importance, creating a non-uniformly compressed model that maximally preserves performance on a target suite of tasks while achieving extreme memory footprint reduction.

ideas.title: Pareto-Optimal Encoder Search for Latency-Throughput Trade-offs
ideas.description: This research proposes a multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) framework that directly optimizes for the throughput-latency Pareto frontier in encoder design. Instead of applying NAS to find a single 'best' encoder, the search space and reward function are designed to discover and characterize architecturally distinct variants—for example, models favoring parallelizable wide layers for throughput versus models with shallow, fast data paths for latency. This results in a discovered continuum or discrete set of model blueprints, each with inherently different block structures, attention mechanisms, and activation functions optimized for its target regime. The outcome is a principled understanding and a generated suite of encoder architectures that provide superior performance within specific deployment constraints, moving beyond one-size-fits-all efficiency optimizations.

ideas.title: Disentangling Data, Model, and Compute Scaling Laws for Modern Encoders
ideas.description: This research conducts a comprehensive multi-dimensional scaling analysis for contemporary encoder architectures. By independently varying training dataset size, model parameter count, and computational budget across several leading encoder designs, it isolates the unique and interactive contributions of each factor to final performance. The study aims to produce a set of fundamental scaling relationships that distinguish data-limited from compute-limited regimes and reveal architectural efficiencies. This framework provides robust, generalizable principles for resource allocation in encoder pre-training, moving beyond single-architecture observations.

ideas.title: A Unified Compiler Framework for Hardware-Agnostic Transformer Inference
ideas.description: This research designs a single, unified compiler stack that takes a standard model definition (like ModernBERT) and automatically optimizes it for diverse inference accelerators. The core innovation is a hardware-agnostic intermediate representation (IR) that captures high-level computational patterns, coupled with a cost model and search-based compiler that generates highly efficient, specialized kernel code and execution graphs for any target backend. This approach eliminates per-hardware manual engineering, future-proofs the model against new accelerators, and allows for joint optimization across the model algorithm and hardware constraints.
